analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Khamzat Chimaev -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH** (9 UFC fights, full career splits available). **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Hybrid.** Direction tag: **FORWARD.** Primary weapon is elite-level wrestling pressure — 5.29 TD/15min on 55% accuracy with 85% TD defense; secondary weapon is submission finishing (4 subs in last 9 fights). Plan A is wall-and-wrestle, Plan B is ground-and-pound + choke.

- **Striking severely suppressed by wrestling game plan.** Career SLpM is 4.04, but last-3 splits show 0.04, 0.84, and 1.07 — an average of ~0.65/min. This is not a striker who wrestles; this is a wrestler who occasionally strikes. The striking career average inflates from early, weaker opponents. Against elite competition (Usman, Whittaker, Du Plessis), SLpM collapsed to near-zero because he commits to the takedown game entirely.

- **WEIGHT CUT RISK flagged.** Three career weight misses on record. Per Rule 9.3 — apply −5% WEIGHT-COMPROMISED tag. Fighter is likely at the top of MW's natural limit. R3+ cardio against Du Plessis (won by U-DEC R5) showed he can grind but was not dominant late — his SLpM in that fight was 0.04, suggesting near-zero output by the championship rounds.

- **Grappling trend is elite but last-3 shows variance.** TD/15 in last-3: 0.6 (vs. DDP), 8.41 (vs. Whittaker), 2.0 (vs. Usman). The 0.6 against Du Plessis in a 5-round title fight is notable — elite opponents wall off his takedowns at high rates. TRENDING: TD volume is FLAT to career average overall, but against the absolute elite it compresses.

- **Intangible: 266-day layoff.** Per Rule 9.2 — 180–270 days = mild ring rust risk, −3% penalty applied. Not severe, but notable.

- **Age 32, undefeated at 15-0.** No KO losses, no chin concerns — no CHIN CRACKED flag. No late-round stoppages pattern. IRON CHIN: insufficient data to formally confirm (never been properly tested on the feet), but no durability red flags.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Sean Strickland -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH** (10 UFC fights tracked, full career splits). **Archetype: Volume Striker / Counter-Striker hybrid.** Direction tag: **BACKWARD.** Career 6.04 SLpM with 42% accuracy and 60% strike defense — he throws a high volume of jabs and straights from a high-guard defensive shell. Rarely initiates explosively; pulls opponents into exchanges and counters off the back foot. 76% TDD (career) — serviceable but not elite.

- **STRIKING VOLUME SEVERELY TRENDING DOWN in last-3.** Career SLpM: 6.04. Last-3 SLpM: 2.58 (Hernandez), 0.64 (DDP-2), 0.96 (Costa) — last-3 average ≈ 1.39/min. **TRENDING DOWN −77% from career average.** This is not a minor blip — it represents a fundamental shift in output against elite/top-10 competition. The 6.04 career average was built on prelim-level opponents. His elite-opponent baseline is closer to 1.0–2.0 SLpM.

- **Strike defense trending concern.** Career SApM: 4.57 absorbed/min. This is high for a counter-striker claiming defensive value. Against Du Plessis (both fights) and Costa, he absorbed clean shots consistently. The 60% career strike defense is also in decline context: being KO'd by Pereira (R1, clean head kick), the career SApM of 4.57 is a **CHIN DECAY ALERT** signal. Flag: **SApM is high, and he's been KO'd before** (Pereira R1). Per Rule 6.2 checks: 1 KO loss (Pereira, R1 — early stoppage = chin flag, criteria 2 met). Age 35 — MW cliff is 35+, criteria 5 met. **Two criteria met → CHIN CRACKED flagged.**

- **Age 35 — at the MW cliff.** Per Rule 9.7: −5% applied at age 35 for MW. The decline is evident in output data. Two losses to Du Plessis (both decisions) and a KO loss to Pereira suggest he's fighting at the ceiling of what he can achieve at this stage.

- **TDD: 76% career.** Serviceable — sits in the 60–75% "coin flip / lean wrestler" zone per Rule 5.2. Against a pressure wrestler of Chimaev's level, 76% TDD will be tested severely. His last-3 TD attempts allowed: 0.0 TD/15 in all 3 fights, but these were striking-dominant opponents (Hernandez, DDP, Costa) who didn't seriously shoot. This TDD figure is **unvalidated against elite wrestlers.**

- **Recent positive signal: KO of Hernandez (Feb 2026, R3, KO/TKO).** This is the most recent fight — only 77 days ago. Per layoff Rule 9.2: <90 days = no penalty. The Hernandez win shows he still has pop, but Hernandez is unranked opposition. Per Rule 3.2: trending against unranked — discount heavily as a quality signal.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**The style matrix gives a clear structural verdict: Pressure Wrestler vs. Volume/Counter-Striker — the wrestler wins unless TDD > 75%.** Strickland's career TDD is exactly 76% — right at the threshold. Per Rule 5.2, this is a "coin flip, lean wrestler" call on TDD alone. But the key caveat is that Strickland's TDD figure has never been validated against a wrestler of Chimaev's caliber (5.29 TD/15min, 55% accuracy). Chimaev's TDD of 85% means Strickland's rudimentary takedown offense (0.71 TD/15min) is irrelevant — this is functionally a one-direction wrestling battle. Chimaev shoots, Strickland tries to survive or stuffs. When Strickland fails to stuff (which is likely given opponent quality gap), the fight becomes a ground control/submission scenario.

**The striking matchup nominally favors Strickland, but the data doesn't support the reputation.** Strickland's last-3 SLpM against elite competition averages ~1.39/min — barely above Chimaev's suppressed elite-opponent output of ~0.65/min. The "volume striker" identity is largely a prelim construction. More critically, Strickland has been flagged **CHIN CRACKED** (KO loss R1 to Pereira, age 35, SApM 4.57 trending high) while Chimaev carries zero KO-loss history. Even in the striking exchanges that do occur, Chimaev's 60% striking accuracy against the 42% accuracy of Strickland suggests Chimaev's shots land cleaner when they're thrown. The FORWARD pressure of Chimaev's wrestling will also prevent Strickland from establishing the lateral movement and range management that defines his counter-striking game.

**Net intangible delta favors Chimaev meaningfully.** Chimaev: −3% (layoff 266 days) + −5% (weight-compromised, 3 misses). Total: −8%. Strickland: −5% (age 35 cliff). Total: −5%. Net delta: Chimaev carries −3% more in intangible penalties than Strickland. However, the structural edge from wrestling superiority vastly outweighs the intangible gap. Strickland's path to victory is narrow: keep Chimaev at range for 25 minutes using footwork and jab volume, avoid the clinch, stuff every single-leg attempt, and accumulate enough strikes to win a decision. This requires his TDD to hold at 76%+ against an opponent who has averaged 8.41 TD/15min against elite opponents in favorable matchups. Chimaev's path is straightforward: close distance, secure clinch, take down, finish on the ground or grind to a decision via control.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Strickland's validated TDD against elite wrestlers is materially higher than 76% (e.g., based on additional camp reports or grappling-specific preparation), this read softens. If Chimaev's weight-cut issues compromise his gas tank after R2, Strickland's cardio-for-decision path becomes more plausible. If Chimaev's layoff (266 days) creates timing issues in his takedown entries, the striking matchup becomes more competitive for 1–2 rounds.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math anchors:**
- Chimaev base sub rate: 4/15 fights = 26.7%. Against Strickland TDD 76%: adjusted_sub_prob = 0.267 × (1 − 0.76) × (1 − 0.7) = 0.267 × 0.24 × 0.30 ≈ **1.9%** per sub attempt window — but TD probability must be factored first. Estimated TD landing probability: ~45–55% per attempt given quality gap. Once down, submission probability conditional on control is historically ~40% for Chimaev. Combined ground-finish path: ~30–40%.
- Chimaev base KO rate: 2/15 = 13.3%. Strickland str def 60%. chin_modifier = 1.4 (CHIN CRACKED). adjusted_KO_prob = 0.133 × (1 − 0.60) × 1.4 = 0.133 × 0.40 × 1.4 ≈ **7.4%**.
- Strickland base KO rate: 2/10 recent fights = 20%. Chimaev str def 43% (career). Chimaev no chin flag → modifier 1.0. adjusted_KO_prob = 0.20 × (1 − 0.43) × 1.0 = 0.20 × 0.57 ≈ **11.4%** — but Chimaev's anti-striking game plan suppresses exposure significantly. Discount to ~5%.
- Decision prob ≈ 1 − 0.074 − 0.35 − 0.05 − 0.05 ≈ **47%** approximate.

---

**#1 — Khamzat Chimaev wins by Submission, Rounds 1–3**
Probability: 35%
Why: Chimaev's most consistent closing mechanism against elite opponents is the ground game — 4 submissions in 9 fights, including Whittaker and Holland on first-round takedowns. Strickland's TDD of 76% is unvalidated against Chimaev's level of wrestling (5.29 TD/15min, 55% accuracy), and once the fight hits the mat, Strickland's sub defense proxy is weak (near-zero submission defense history). Adjusted sub probability with CHIN CRACKED modifier not applicable here but grappling path remains the primary vehicle. The R1 window is particularly dangerous given Chimaev's history of early finishes.

**#2 — Khamzat Chimaev wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 5**
Probability: 30%
Why: If Strickland survives the early submission threats through active defense and frames, Chimaev may grind to a decision similar to the Du Plessis fight (0.04 SLpM output, control-based). However, Chimaev's ability to secure at least 3–4 takedowns per round (career 5.29 TD/15) with ground control time gives him 3–5 rounds of dominance on the judges' scorecards. This is the "boring Chimaev" outcome — the same one that secured the Du Plessis belt.

**#3 — Khamzat Chimaev wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1–2**
Probability: 12%
Why: Strickland's CHIN CRACKED flag (KO loss to Pereira R1, age 35, SApM 4.57) elevates this scenario above its raw stat baseline. Chimaev's adjusted KO probability comes to ~7.4% from the formula, but the early-round window before Strickland settles adds upside. If Chimaev connects with a clean overhand or follows a taken-down Strickland with ground-and-pound, a TKO stoppage is plausible. Chimaev has shown knockout power (Gerald Meerschaert 0:17 R1, Rhys McKee R1 TKO).

**#4 — Sean Strickland wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 5**
Probability: 13%
Why: Strickland's path requires a 76%+ TDD night, consistent lateral movement to negate cage trapping, and enough output (1.5–2.5 SLpM) to accumulate on the feet. He's done this against elite opponents (Adesanya U-DEC, Costa S-DEC) when the grappling threat was lower. If Chimaev's weight-cut compromises his gas tank in rounds 3–5 and his takedown entries slow, Strickland's jab-heavy counter-striking accumulates points on the cards. This is the longest-shot plausible outcome but not negligible given a full 5-round format.

**#5 — Sean Strickland wins by KO/TKO**
Probability: 5%
Why: Strickland has power (Pereira loss shows he gets hit with power, but he also has stopped 2 opponents recently including Hernandez R3 and Magomedov R2). If Chimaev shoots a sloppy takedown and gets caught coming in by a right hand or knee, the fight can end unexpectedly. Historical precedent: power strikers occasionally catch wrestlers mid-shot. Low probability but non-zero given Strickland's improved KO rate in last-2.

*Total: 35 + 30 + 12 + 13 + 5 = 95% (5% covers unlisted outcomes like draws, no contest, or other stoppage scenarios)*
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**1. TIER LABEL: LEAN**

**2. PRIMARY PICK: Khamzat Chimaev | Confidence: 62%**

*(Note: Confidence capped per Rule 13.4 — while both fighters are HIGH tier, the WEIGHT CUT RISK on Chimaev and the 3-weight-miss history introduce a meaningful uncertainty layer. Additionally, the line has moved dramatically from −400 to −590, eroding value significantly. Confidence on the outcome is ~68–70% before market discounting, but value confidence is capped at LEAN given the price.)*

---

**3. EDGE BREAKDOWN:**

- **Chimaev line (current): −590**
- Implied probability (−590): 590 / (590 + 100) = **85.5%**
- **Strickland line (current): +300**
- Implied probability (+300): 100 / (300 + 100) = **25.0%**
- Combined implied: 85.5% + 25.0% = **110.5%** (10.5% vig — extremely high)
- De-vig true prob Chimaev: 85.5 / 110.5 = **77.4%**
- De-vig true prob Strickland: 25.0 / 110.5 = **22.6%**
- **Assessed Chimaev win probability: 68%**
- **Edge on Chimaev: 68% − 77.4% = −9.4% (NEGATIVE — no value on Chimaev at this price)**
- **Assessed Strickland win probability: 32%**
- **Edge on Strickland: 32% − 22.6% = +9.4% (POSITIVE — value exists on Strickland)**

The value is on **Strickland** as the underdog, not Chimaev. The line has moved so far toward Chimaev that the market is now over-pricing him relative to the structural read.

**Revised Primary Pick: Sean Strickland +300 | Assessed Prob: 32% | Market True Prob: 22.6% | Edge: +9.4%**

---

**4. STAKE:**
- Strickland decimal odds: (300/100) + 1 = 4.00
- Kelly fraction: (0.094 × 4.00 − (1 − 0.32)) / (4.00 − 1) = (0.376 − 0.68) / 3.0 = −0.304 / 3.0 = **−0.101**
- Quarter Kelly: −0.101 × 0.25 = **negative (−0.025)**

*Mathematically, even with +9.4% edge vs. de-vigged market, the Kelly formula at +300 (4.00 decimal) goes negative because the assessed win probability of 32% is below the "break-even" for a 4.0 decimal bet (which requires 25% to be +EV, but full Kelly requires ~27.5% — the margin is thin enough that quarter Kelly goes negative).*

**Recheck:** Full Kelly formula for +EV: k = (b×p − q) / b where b = 3.0 (net profit multiple), p = 0.32, q = 0.68.
k = (3.0 × 0.32 − 0.68) / 3.0 = (0.96 − 0.68) / 3.0 = 0.28 / 3.0 = **0.093 (9.3% of bankroll)**
Quarter Kelly: 0.093 × 0.25 = **0.023 = 2.3% of bankroll ≈ 0.7 units**

*The earlier error was using a net decimal vs. gross decimal. Corrected: stake recommendation = **0.7 units.***

---

**5. TARGET LINE:**
- Minimum line for +3% edge on Strickland (requiring true prob ≤ 29% to keep edge ≥ 3%): approximately **+225 or better**
- Current line (+300) is above target — value exists at current price.
- **Target line: +225 (worst acceptable price for this bet)**

---

**6. DATA TIERS:**
- Chimaev: **HIGH tier** (9 UFC fights, full splits, recent activity)
- Strickland: **HIGH tier** (10 UFC fights tracked, full splits)
- Active confidence caps: WEIGHT CUT RISK on Chimaev (Rule 9.3) reduces overall model confidence. No formal cap rule fires at LOW/MEDIUM tier, but the weight issue introduces a conservatism discount.

---

**7. MARKET READ:**
- **Chimaev open: −400 → current: −590** (shortened by 190 cents — massive public/sharp money on Chimaev)
- **Strickland open: +490 → current: +300** (lengthened — Strickland has become shorter as an underdog since opening, meaning money came IN on Chimaev hard)
- Wait — re-reading: Strickland opened −490? That appears to be a data error in the input (both fighters listed as favorites simultaneously is impossible). Most likely the Strickland opening line was **+490** (not −490), and it has **shortened to +300**, meaning sharp/public money has come IN on Strickland since opening.
- **Interpretation of corrected read:** Chimaev opened −400 (Chimaev is the favorite). Strickland opened +490 and has shortened to +300 — the line on Strickland has **shortened by ~180 cents**, meaning money has been placed ON Strickland, moving him from +490 to +300.
- **RLM status: Labeled NO in data.** However, Chimaev's line shortening from −400 to −590 suggests heavy Chimaev money as well. Both sides have seen action — no confirmed RLM.
- **Assessment:** The line movement toward Strickland (from +490 to +300) is a mild sharp signal worth noting. Strickland attracting enough money to move from +490 to +300 on a fight where the public almost certainly favors Chimaev (undefeated, bigger name) is a mild RLM-adjacent signal — at minimum, sharp money has found value on Strickland. This aligns with our edge calculation. Per Rule 10.11: stat-read agrees with market direction on Strickland → mild +5% confidence support (capped at confirmation, not formal RLM).

---

**8. HEDGE VERDICT:**
- Best available: Chimaev −590 / Strickland +300
- Combined implied: 85.5% + 25.0% = **110.5%**
- **NO ARB.** Combined implied is well above 100%. No hedge opportunity. Take the value pick (Strickland) on the best available line.

---

**9. PROPS WORTH CONSIDERING:**

**A. "Fight goes to Decision" YES (if available)**
- Section 8.4 decision prob estimate: ~47%
- If the decision prop is priced below ~47% implied (i.e., offered at worse than −89 YES / +140-ish), there is value on YES.
- This is consistent with Chimaev's recent title-fight pattern (beat Du Plessis by decision, beat Usman by decision) and Strickland's high decision rate (4 of last 6 wins were decisions).
- **Flag as a prop worth seeking.** De-vig any offering before acting.

**B. "Chimaev wins by Submission" (if available)**
- Adjusted sub probability: ~35% (derived above)
- If this prop is offered below −90 implied (~47% de-vigged), fade it. If offered at +120 or longer, there may be value.
- Most books will price this conservatively given his history — check the exact line.

---

**10. FADE ALERT:**
- **Initial lean was toward Chimaev** (structural wrestling favorite). After de-vig math, the market has overpriced Chimaev at −590 (77.4% true prob) vs. assessed 68%. **Switching recommendation to Strickland +300 per Rule 13.8.** This is a fade-the-price play, not a fade-the-fighter play — Chimaev is still likely to win, but not at −590.

---

**SUMMARY:**
> **LEAN: Sean Strickland +300 for 0.7 units.**
> Assessed win probability: 32%. Market true prob: 22.6%. Edge: +9.4%. Quarter-Kelly: 0.7 units. Target line: +225 (current +300 offers value). Data tier: both HIGH. Market read: Strickland has shortened from +490 to +300, suggesting some sharp action on his side. No ARB available. This is a lean on underdog value, not a conviction play — Chimaev remains the correct structural favorite; the bet is that the market has over-adjusted his price beyond what the true probability warrants.

**#3 — Khamzat Chimaev wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1–2**
Probability: 12%
Why: Strickland's CHIN CRACKED flag (KO loss to Pereira R1, age 35, SApM 4.57) elevates this scenario above its raw stat baseline. Chimaev's adjusted KO probability comes to ~7.4% from the formula, but the early-round window before Strickland settles adds upside. If Chimaev connects with a clean overhand or follows a taken-down Strickland with ground-and-pound, a TKO stoppage is plausible. Chimaev has shown knockout power (Gerald Meerschaert 0:17 R1, Rhys McKee R1 TKO).

**#4 — Sean Strickland wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 5**
Probability: 13%
Why: Strickland's path requires a 76%+ TDD night, consistent lateral movement to negate cage trapping, and enough output (1.5–2.5 SLpM) to accumulate on the feet. He's done this against elite opponents (Adesanya U-DEC, Costa S-DEC) when the grappling threat was lower. If Chimaev's weight-cut compromises his gas tank in rounds 3–5 and his takedown entries slow, Strickland's jab-heavy counter-striking accumulates points on the cards. This is the longest-shot plausible outcome but not negligible given a full 5-round format.

**#5 — Sean Strickland wins by KO/TKO**
Probability: 5%
Why: Strickland has power (Pereira loss shows he gets hit with power, but he also has stopped 2 opponents recently including Hernandez R3 and Magomedov R2). If Chimaev shoots a sloppy takedown and gets caught coming in by a right hand or knee, the fight can end unexpectedly. Historical precedent: power strikers occasionally catch wrestlers mid-shot. Low probability but non-zero given Strickland's improved KO rate in last-2.

*Total: 35 + 30 + 12 + 13 + 5 = 95% (5% covers unlisted outcomes like draws, no contest, or other stoppage scenarios)*
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
## Betting Recommendation

**1. TIER LABEL: LEAN**

**2. PRIMARY PICK: Khamzat Chimaev | Confidence: 62%**

*(Note: Confidence capped per Rule 13.4 — while both fighters are HIGH tier, the WEIGHT CUT RISK on Chimaev and the 3-weight-miss history introduce a meaningful uncertainty layer. Additionally, the line has moved dramatically from −400 to −590, eroding value significantly. Confidence on the outcome is ~68–70% before market discounting, but value confidence is capped at LEAN given the price.)*

---

**3. EDGE BREAKDOWN:**

- **Chimaev line (current): −590**
- Implied probability (−590): 590 / (590 + 100) = **85.5%**
- **Strickland line (current): +300**
- Implied probability (+300): 100 / (300 + 100) = **25.0%**
- Combined implied: 85.5% + 25.0% = **110.5%** (10.5% vig — extremely high)
- De-vig true prob Chimaev: 85.5 / 110.5 = **77.4%**
- De-vig true prob Strickland: 25.0 / 110.5 = **22.6%**
- **Assessed Chimaev win probability: 68%**
- **Edge on Chimaev: 68% − 77.4% = −9.4% (NEGATIVE — no value on Chimaev at this price)**
- **Assessed Strickland win probability: 32%**
- **Edge on Strickland: 32% − 22.6% = +9.4% (POSITIVE — value exists on Strickland)**

The value is on **Strickland** as the underdog, not Chimaev. The line has moved so far toward Chimaev that the market is now over-pricing him relative to the structural read.

**Revised Primary Pick: Sean Strickland +300 | Assessed Prob: 32% | Market True Prob: 22.6% | Edge: +9.4%**

---

**4. STAKE:**
- Strickland decimal odds: (300/100) + 1 = 4.00
- Kelly fraction: (0.094 × 4.00 − (1 − 0.32)) / (4.00 − 1) = (0.376 − 0.68) / 3.0 = −0.304 / 3.0 = **−0.101**
- Quarter Kelly: −0.101 × 0.25 = **negative (−0.025)**

*Mathematically, even with +9.4% edge vs. de-vigged market, the Kelly formula at +300 (4.00 decimal) goes negative because the assessed win probability of 32% is below the "break-even" for a 4.0 decimal bet (which requires 25% to be +EV, but full Kelly requires ~27.5% — the margin is thin enough that quarter Kelly goes negative).*

**Recheck:** Full Kelly formula for +EV: k = (b×p − q) / b where b = 3.0 (net profit multiple), p = 0.32, q = 0.68.
k = (3.0 × 0.32 − 0.68) / 3.0 = (0.96 − 0.68) / 3.0 = 0.28 / 3.0 = **0.093 (9.3% of bankroll)**
Quarter Kelly: 0.093 × 0.25 = **0.023 = 2.3% of bankroll ≈ 0.7 units**

*The earlier error was using a net decimal vs. gross decimal. Corrected: stake recommendation = **0.7 units.***

---

**5. TARGET LINE:**
- Minimum line for +3% edge on Strickland (requiring true prob ≤ 29% to keep edge ≥ 3%): approximately **+225 or better**
- Current line (+300) is above target — value exists at current price.
- **Target line: +225 (worst acceptable price for this bet)**

---

**6. DATA TIERS:**
- Chimaev: **HIGH tier** (9 UFC fights, full splits, recent activity)
- Strickland: **HIGH tier** (10 UFC fights tracked, full splits)
- Active confidence caps: WEIGHT CUT RISK on Chimaev (Rule 9.3) reduces overall model confidence. No formal cap rule fires at LOW/MEDIUM tier, but the weight issue introduces a conservatism discount.

---

**7. MARKET READ:**
- **Chimaev open: −400 → current: −590** (shortened by 190 cents — massive public/sharp money on Chimaev)
- **Strickland open: +490 → current: +300** (lengthened — Strickland has become shorter as an underdog since opening, meaning money came IN on Chimaev hard)
- Wait — re-reading: Strickland opened −490? That appears to be a data error in the input (both fighters listed as favorites simultaneously is impossible). Most likely the Strickland opening line was **+490** (not −490), and it has **shortened to +300**, meaning sharp/public money has come IN on Strickland since opening.
- **Interpretation of corrected read:** Chimaev opened −400 (Chimaev is the favorite). Strickland opened +490 and has shortened to +300 — the line on Strickland has **shortened by ~180 cents**, meaning money has been placed ON Strickland, moving him from +490 to +300.
- **RLM status: Labeled NO in data.** However, Chimaev's line shortening from −400 to −590 suggests heavy Chimaev money as well. Both sides have seen action — no confirmed RLM.
- **Assessment:** The line movement toward Strickland (from +490 to +300) is a mild sharp signal worth noting. Strickland attracting enough money to move from +490 to +300 on a fight where the public almost certainly favors Chimaev (undefeated, bigger name) is a mild RLM-adjacent signal — at minimum, sharp money has found value on Strickland. This aligns with our edge calculation. Per Rule 10.11: stat-read agrees with market direction on Strickland → mild +5% confidence support (capped at confirmation, not formal RLM).

---

**8. HEDGE VERDICT:**
- Best available: Chimaev −590 / Strickland +300
- Combined implied: 85.5% + 25.0% = **110.5%**
- **NO ARB.** Combined implied is well above 100%. No hedge opportunity. Take the value pick (Strickland) on the best available line.

---

**9. PROPS WORTH CONSIDERING:**

**A. "Fight goes to Decision" YES (if available)**
- Section 8.4 decision prob estimate: ~47%
- If the decision prop is priced below ~47% implied (i.e., offered at worse than −89 YES / +140-ish), there is value on YES.
- This is consistent with Chimaev's recent title-fight pattern (beat Du Plessis by decision, beat Usman by decision) and Strickland's high decision rate (4 of last 6 wins were decisions).
- **Flag as a prop worth seeking.** De-vig any offering before acting.

**B. "Chimaev wins by Submission" (if available)**
- Adjusted sub probability: ~35% (derived above)
- If this prop is offered below −90 implied (~47% de-vigged), fade it. If offered at +120 or longer, there may be value.
- Most books will price this conservatively given his history — check the exact line.

---

**10. FADE ALERT:**
- **Initial lean was toward Chimaev** (structural wrestling favorite). After de-vig math, the market has overpriced Chimaev at −590 (77.4% true prob) vs. assessed 68%. **Switching recommendation to Strickland +300 per Rule 13.8.** This is a fade-the-price play, not a fade-the-fighter play — Chimaev is still likely to win, but not at −590.

---

**SUMMARY:**
> **LEAN: Sean Strickland +300 for 0.7 units.**
> Assessed win probability: 32%. Market true prob: 22.6%. Edge: +9.4%. Quarter-Kelly: 0.7 units. Target line: +225 (current +300 offers value). Data tier: both HIGH. Market read: Strickland has shortened from +490 to +300, suggesting some sharp action on his side. No ARB available. This is a lean on underdog value, not a conviction play — Chimaev remains the correct structural favorite; the bet is that the market has over-adjusted his price beyond what the true probability warrants.
<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Joshua Van -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH** (7+ UFC fights, full per-fight splits, active — last fight 154 days ago). **Archetype: Volume Striker trending toward Power Striker.** Career SLpM of 8.84 is elite-tier volume for flyweight; strike accuracy at 56% is above average for that pace. **Direction: FORWARD.**

- **Striking output is elite but last-3 splits show a significant volume drop:** Career SLpM 8.84 vs. last-3 average ~3.36 (4.62 + 3.47 + 2.0 / 3) — **TRENDING DOWN (−62%)**. This is a massive divergence. The career number appears inflated by earlier UFC fights; his recent fights show he is operating at a far lower pace. Accuracy is holding (50–66% in last-3), suggesting he's fighting more selectively, not declining — but the volume signal cannot be ignored.

- **KO power is real and recent:** KO'd Alexandre Pantoja (then-champion) in 26 seconds in his most recent fight, and KO'd Bruno Silva in R3. 3 KOs in last 10 fights. When he lands clean, he finishes — but the finish rate against elite competition is still building.

- **Grappling is almost nonexistent in recent fights:** TD Avg career 0.84/15min; last-3 show 0.0 takedowns per fight across all three fights. Sub attempts 0.0/15min career and last-3. He is a pure striker who does not initiate grappling. **TDD at 81% is elite** — the highest-leverage defensive stat in this matchup.

- **WEIGHT-COMPROMISED:** 2 historical weight misses. Per Rule 9.3, this flag is active. Combined with career SApM of 6.39 (high absorption rate even at flyweight), there is a durability question — he gets hit a lot when he does engage. **CHIN ALERT (borderline):** 1 KO loss on record (Charles Johnson, R3 stoppage), SApM at 6.39 career (TRENDING DOWN in last-3 to ~4.3, which is somewhat reassuring), but the absorption rate remains high relative to the division.

- **Only career loss came via R3 KO** (Charles Johnson) — a late stoppage, suggesting cardio/durability in extended fights is the historical vulnerability. His recent 6-fight win streak includes three R3 decisions, indicating he can go the distance, but that Johnson loss is the lone blot.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Tatsuro Taira -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH** (9 UFC fights, full per-fight splits, active — last fight 154 days ago). **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Submission Specialist.** Career TD Avg 3.12/15min, Sub Avg 1.6/15min, 3 subs in last 9 fights. Striking is a tool to set up takedowns, not a primary weapon. **Direction: FORWARD** (enters, closes distance, initiates grappling pressure).

- **Last-3 splits show a striking-light, grappling-dominant fighter — but the grappling numbers collapsed in the last-3 sample:** Career TD Avg 3.12/15min vs. last-3 average ~1.84/15min (0.0 + 4.92 + 0.6 / 3) — **TRENDING DOWN (−41%)** dragged by the Moreno (0.0 TDs) and Royval (0.6 TDs) fights. The Park fight (4.92 TDs) is the outlier high. His ability to get takedowns is inconsistent against high-level opposition.

- **Striking defense is a liability: 47% career strike defense** — below average for flyweight. Against Van's 8.84 career SLpM (even if last-3 is lower, Van has clear KO power), Taira will absorb significant volume if the fight stays standing. SApM of 2.44 is low because he doesn't often stand long — but when he does, he gets hit at concerning rates.

- **Takedown accuracy is mediocre: 46% TD Acc career.** This means he's shooting and missing roughly half the time. Against Van's 81% TDD, the math becomes very unfavorable (see HEAD2HEAD). His submission game is real when he gets there, but the path to the floor against this opponent is the central question.

- **WEIGHT-COMPROMISED:** 2 historical weight misses. Same flag as Van — both fighters have this issue, so it's neutral as a comparative edge, but flags R3 cardio reliability for both.

- **Win over Brandon Moreno (KO, R2) is legitimate elite-level evidence;** however, the Royval split-decision loss (R5) in a 5-round fight exposed his cardio and grappling output decline — he was outworked in later rounds. The Moreno finish came via strikes after a takedown was secured, suggesting his power is real in transition, not as a pure stand-up striker (SLpM 0.27 in that fight — almost no striking output, the finish came off a specific sequence).
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**The central variable in this matchup is Taira's ability to take Van down, and the math is brutal for Taira.** Per Rule 5.2, TDD is the master variable in a striker-vs-wrestler matchup. Van's TDD is 81% — well above the 75% threshold where a striker can fight his own fight and the matrix structural advantage holds for the striker. Taira's TD accuracy is 46% against average competition; against a fighter defending at 81%, the adjusted takedown probability per attempt drops substantially. Using a rough estimate: 46% base accuracy × (1 − 0.81 defender advantage weight) implies Taira may successfully land only ~1 in 5–6 serious attempts against Van. The style matrix reads: **Pressure Wrestler vs. Volume Striker with TDD > 75% → Striker holds structural advantage.** This is not a comfortable position for Taira.

**Van's striking edge is real but comes with a serious caveat: his last-3 volume (avg ~3.36 SLpM) diverges massively from his career number (8.84 SLpM), and his strike defense is only 57%.** Taira's 47% strike defense means both fighters are hittable, but Van is the more dangerous striker by a significant margin. Van's reach deficit (65" vs. Taira's 70") is a genuine concern — a 5-inch disadvantage (Rule 5.3: ≥4 inches is a real edge) means Taira has measurable range control if he chooses to use it. However, Taira's striking output in recent fights has been minimal (0.27 SLpM vs. Moreno, 0.98 vs. Park, 0.4 vs. Royval) — he is not a striker who exploits reach; he closes distance and grapples. This neutralizes the reach advantage somewhat because Taira is not a counter-striker at range.

**The fight decision point is Round 1–2 grappling exchanges.** If Taira cannot secure takedowns early, he has no credible path. Van, fighting forward and with clear KO power (Pantoja in 26 seconds is the most recent data point against elite-tier opposition), becomes progressively more dangerous the longer the fight stays standing. Taira's loss to Royval — a split-decision in R5 — and his visible output decline late in that fight suggest his cardio past Round 2–3 is a question mark. Van's losses have also been late (Johnson, R3), but he's been much more active recently. **Net intangible delta:** Both fighters have 2 weight misses — neutral. Van is 2 years younger (24 vs. 26) — negligible at these ages. Layoffs are identical (154 days). The intangibles are largely a wash; the matchup math drives this one.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Taira's TD accuracy in this fight exceeds 50% in R1 (i.e., he finds the level change early), switch to Taira — his submission game off the back is legitimate. If Van's strike defense holds but his volume drops below 2.0 SLpM again (as in the Silva fight), the fight becomes a grappling contest Van doesn't want. If Taira's reach advantage manifests in a jab-heavy game plan (not historically his pattern), it could suppress Van's offense more than the model suggests.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Joshua Van wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 32%
Why: Van has demonstrated elite-level KO power against top competition (Pantoja, R1; Silva, R3). Taira's 47% strike defense (below average) and forward-closing grappling style mean he will walk into strikes at close range while shooting. Using Section 8.2 math: base KO rate Van ~30% of UFC fights (3 KOs in ~10 fights) × (1 − 0.47 Taira str def) = 30% × 0.53 = ~15.9%; adjusted upward for Taira's tendency to close distance and the chin_modifier (Taira has taken some clean shots in career, no formal CHIN CRACKED flag but no IRON CHIN either — use 1.1 modifier) = ~17.5%. Bumped further by the fight being likely 3 rounds (undercard format) reducing cardio factors and the Pantoja precedent. Rounded to 32% accounting for style dynamic.

**#2 — Tatsuro Taira wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 22%
Why: Taira's submission game is legitimate — 3 subs in 9 UFC fights, Sub Avg 1.6/15min. Per Section 8.3: base sub rate ~33% of fights × (1 − 0.81 Van TDD) × (1 − 0.3 sub def proxy) = 33% × 0.19 × 0.70 = ~4.4% per minute of fight time, but this underweights the scenario where Taira *does* get a takedown. If he successfully secures even one takedown (which at 46% accuracy and 81% TDD defense implies roughly 1 successful TD per 4–5 attempts), his submission threat from top position or back-take is real. The path is narrow but not negligible. Most of his sub wins have come in Rounds 1–2 before cardio issues emerge.

**#3 — Joshua Van wins by unanimous decision**
Probability: 21%
Why: Van's last three fights include two decision wins (Royval, Tsuruya). If Taira cannot solve the 81% TDD to get the fight to the floor, Van controls striking exchanges from range (despite the reach disadvantage, he's comfortable at distance). Using Section 8.4: decision prob ≈ 1 − 0.32 (Van KO) − 0.22 (Taira sub) − 0.08 (Taira KO, below) − 0.05 (Van sub, negligible at 0.0/15min) ≈ 33% total decision probability; split roughly 21% Van / 12% scenario split decision, reflecting Van's striking edge on the feet.

**#4 — Tatsuro Taira wins by KO/TKO, Round 2**
Probability: 8%
Why: The Moreno finish (R2, 2:24) proves Taira has finishing power in transition — off a secured takedown or scramble, he can land damaging shots. Van's SApM at 6.39 career (even if trending down in last-3) indicates he absorbs significant volume when fully engaged. Section 8.2 applied to Taira: base KO rate ~33% of fights × (1 − 0.57 Van str def) = 33% × 0.43 = ~14.2%; chin modifier (Van's single KO loss, not CHIN CRACKED, but not IRON CHIN — neutral 1.0) = ~14.2%, but Van's TDD suppressing the grappling entries that enable Taira's KO setups reduces this to ~8%.

**#5 — Decision goes to judge's cards (split or unanimous for either fighter)**
Probability: 12% (residual)
Why: Both fighters are capable of going three rounds — Van has done it repeatedly, Taira's Royval fight went five rounds. If the fight becomes a scramble-heavy contest where neither can impose their game plan decisively, judges decide. Given both fighters' WEIGHT-COMPROMISED flags, R3 pace may drop for both, creating a grinding final round. Probability is residual from the above calculations.

*Total assigned: 95%. Remaining 5% covers disqualification, no-contest, or unusual stoppages.*
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**TIER: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Joshua Van | Confidence: 61%**

---

### Edge Breakdown

**Line:** Joshua Van +155 (current)
**Implied probability (raw):** 100 / (155 + 100) = **39.2%**

**Taira line:** −185 (current)
**Implied probability (raw):** 185 / (185 + 100) = **64.9%**

**De-vig (Rule 11.2):**
Sum = 39.2% + 64.9% = 104.1% (4.1% vig)
- True implied prob Van = 39.2 / 104.1 = **37.7%**
- True implied prob Taira = 64.9 / 104.1 = **62.3%**

**My assessed probability (Van):** 61%
**Edge:** 61% − 37.7% = **+23.3 percentage points**

⚠️ **Rule 13.2 / 13.7 WARNING — Edge > 12%: Re-check required before publishing.**

Re-check performed:
- Van's 81% TDD vs. Taira's 46% TD accuracy: confirmed structural striker advantage per matrix.
- Van's recent KO win over Pantoja (R1, 26 seconds): confirmed elite-level finish power vs. the prior champion — not a can.
- Taira's SApM pathway to victory is narrow: he must successfully take down an 81% TDD fighter to win.
- Taira's own striking stats (0.27 SLpM vs. Moreno, 0.4 vs. Royval, 0.98 vs. Park in last-3) show he is not a striker who can outwork Van on the feet.
- The market has moved from +160 to +155 on Van (slight shortening — sharps slightly preferring Van relative to open), while Taira moved from +155 to −185, representing a 30-cent move in Taira's favor driven primarily by public money on the #1 ranked flyweight contender/story narrative (Taira is the hot name post-Moreno). **No RLM confirmed.**
- The gap between my assessed 61% and the market's 37.7% true prob is legitimate: the market appears to be heavily pricing Taira's brand/narrative (coming off a huge win over Moreno, ranked contender) while under-pricing the specific structural problem his wrestling faces against Van's elite TDD.
- **Adjusted confidence remains at 61%.** The edge is genuine, though toward the upper end; this is a Rule 10.10 situation (returning near-champion-tier hype inflating the line — Taira's Moreno win has the public overreacting).

**Conclusion on edge magnitude:** The edge is large but defensible. The market is pricing a "Taira is a future champion" narrative; the stats price a specific matchup problem. Confidence stays at 61%, tier stays at PLAY.

---

### Stake Calculation (Quarter Kelly — Rule 11.4)

Decimal odds on Van +155: (155/100) + 1 = **2.55**

Kelly fraction = (edge% × decimal_odds − (1 − assessed_prob)) / (decimal_odds − 1)
= (0.233 × 2.55 − (1 − 0.61)) / (2.55 − 1)
= (0.594 − 0.39) / 1.55
= 0.204 / 1.55
= **0.132 (full Kelly)**

Quarter Kelly = 0.132 × 0.25 = **0.033 → 3.3%** of bankroll, or approximately **1.3 units**

Capped at 3 units per Rule 11.5. Recommended stake: **1.3 units.**

**Target line:** The worst price at which the bet still has +3% edge:
- Need: assessed prob − de-vigged true prob ≥ 0.03
- At what Van line does de-vigged true prob = 61% − 3% = 58%?
- If Van is at +110: implied = 47.6%; Taira at −130: implied = 56.5%; sum = 104.1%; de-vigged Van = 47.6/104.1 = 45.7% → still has edge at +110? 61% − 45.7% = +15.3% — yes.
- The line would need to move all the way to approximately Van −110 / −120 before the edge shrinks to 3%. **Target line: Van up to −110 (i.e., any positive-money line or short negative is acceptable).**

---

### Data Tiers

- **Joshua Van: HIGH** (7 UFC fights, full splits, recent activity)
- **Tatsuro Taira: HIGH** (9 UFC fights, full splits, recent activity)
- **No confidence caps triggered from data tier.** Confidence (61%) is within PLAY range for two HIGH-tier fighters.
- **Active intangible: WEIGHT-COMPROMISED on both fighters** (2 weight misses each — Rule 9.3 flags for both, but symmetric — nets to zero comparative delta).

---

### Market Read

- **Van open: +160 → current: +155** (shortened by ~5 cents — slight sharp action on Van, minor)
- **Taira open: +155 → current: −185** (shortened by ~34 cents — significant public/market movement to Taira post-Moreno win narrative)
- **Movement direction:** Heavily toward Taira, consistent with public narrative betting on Taira's momentum.
- **RLM:** NO — line has moved in direction of public favorite (Taira). No reverse line movement.
- **Public %:** Not available — estimated heavily public-sided on Taira (ranked contender, recent viral KO win over Moreno).
- **Market read vs. stat read:** Market disagrees with stat-based read (market heavily favors Taira; model favors Van). Per Rule 10.11: stat-read disagrees with market direction → −5% confidence penalty. **Adjusted confidence: 56%.** Tier revises from PLAY to borderline LEAN.

**Revised recommendation: LEAN to PLAY (56% confidence — above 55% threshold).**

Given the −5% market disagreement penalty bringing confidence to 56%, this is at the boundary of LEAN (55–60%). The edge remains large (+18.3pp after adjustment: 56% − 37.7% = +18.3%), the structural read is sound, and the market move is narrative-driven (not RLM-confirmed sharp money). Recommending as a **modest PLAY at 1.0 unit** (reducing from 1.3 units to reflect market disagreement signal).

---

### Hedge / Arb Analysis (Rule 12.5)

Best available lines assumed from data provided:
- Van best available: +155
- Taira best available: −185

Combined implied = 39.2% + 64.9% = **104.1%**

**VERDICT: NO ARB** — combined implied exceeds 100% (4.1% vig gap). No guaranteed profit structure available. Take the value pick on the best Van line. Do not hedge.

---

### Props Worth Considering

**"Fight goes to decision" — lean NO (Under on decision prop):**
Per Section 8.4 calc: total decision probability ≈ 33% (Van + Taira decision scenarios combined). If the prop market has this at YES −130 or shorter (implied ~56.5%), the model's 33% decision probability suggests value on **NO / Fight does not go to decision**, implying a finish is more likely (~67% finish probability combined). De-vig required — if NO is priced at +110 (implied ~47.6%), de-vigged true prob = ~46%, and model says ~67% finish probability → value on fight ends before decision. **Conditional recommendation: if "Fight ends before decision" or "Fight does NOT go to decision" is available at or better than +100, it has model-implied value. Stake 0.5 units.**

**"Van wins by KO/TKO" — check availability:** Model assigns 32% probability to this outcome. If priced at +300 or longer (implied ~25%), there is value. At +200 (implied ~33.3%), it's roughly break-even after de-vig — pass.

---

### Full Recommendation Summary

```
Pick:          Joshua Van ML
Line:          +155 (best available)
Implied prob:  39.2% raw / 37.7% de-vigged
Assessed prob: 61% base → 56% after market penalty
Edge:          +18.3pp (post-adjustment)
Stake:         1.0 unit (quarter-Kelly, market-discount reduced)
Target line:   Any line from +155 to −110 retains value
Data tiers:    Van HIGH / Taira HIGH
Caps:          −5% market disagreement penalty applied
Arb verdict:   NO ARB
Prop note:     "Fight ends before decision" at +100 or better = 0.5 units
```

**Would I bet $1,000 of my own money?** The structural read is clean (81% TDD vs. 46% TD accuracy is the most important number in this fight), the KO precedent against Pantoja is elite-level evidence, and the market is pricing a narrative. Yes — at 1 unit, this clears the gut-check threshold.

**Fade alert:** None — initial read and final recommendation are aligned (Van).

This is a massive divergence. The career number appears inflated by earlier UFC fights; his recent fights show he is operating at a far lower pace. Accuracy is holding (50–66% in last-3), suggesting he's fighting more selectively, not declining — but the volume signal cannot be ignored.

- **KO power is real and recent:** KO'd Alexandre Pantoja (then-champion) in 26 seconds in his most recent fight, and KO'd Bruno Silva in R3. 3 KOs in last 10 fights. When he lands clean, he finishes — but the finish rate against elite competition is still building.

- **Grappling is almost nonexistent in recent fights:** TD Avg career 0.84/15min; last-3 show 0.0 takedowns per fight across all three fights. Sub attempts 0.0/15min career and last-3. He is a pure striker who does not initiate grappling. **TDD at 81% is elite** — the highest-leverage defensive stat in this matchup.

- **WEIGHT-COMPROMISED:** 2 historical weight misses. Per Rule 9.3, this flag is active. Combined with career SApM of 6.39 (high absorption rate even at flyweight), there is a durability question — he gets hit a lot when he does engage. **CHIN ALERT (borderline):** 1 KO loss on record (Charles Johnson, R3 stoppage), SApM at 6.39 career (TRENDING DOWN in last-3 to ~4.3, which is somewhat reassuring), but the absorption rate remains high relative to the division.

- **Only career loss came via R3 KO** (Charles Johnson) — a late stoppage, suggesting cardio/durability in extended fights is the historical vulnerability. His recent 6-fight win streak includes three R3 decisions, indicating he can go the distance, but that Johnson loss is the lone blot.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Tatsuro Taira -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: HIGH** (9 UFC fights, full per-fight splits, active — last fight 154 days ago). **Archetype: Pressure Wrestler / Submission Specialist.** Career TD Avg 3.12/15min, Sub Avg 1.6/15min, 3 subs in last 9 fights. Striking is a tool to set up takedowns, not a primary weapon. **Direction: FORWARD** (enters, closes distance, initiates grappling pressure).

- **Last-3 splits show a striking-light, grappling-dominant fighter — but the grappling numbers collapsed in the last-3 sample:** Career TD Avg 3.12/15min vs. last-3 average ~1.84/15min (0.0 + 4.92 + 0.6 / 3) — **TRENDING DOWN (−41%)** dragged by the Moreno (0.0 TDs) and Royval (0.6 TDs) fights. The Park fight (4.92 TDs) is the outlier high. His ability to get takedowns is inconsistent against high-level opposition.

- **Striking defense is a liability: 47% career strike defense** — below average for flyweight. Against Van's 8.84 career SLpM (even if last-3 is lower, Van has clear KO power), Taira will absorb significant volume if the fight stays standing. SApM of 2.44 is low because he doesn't often stand long — but when he does, he gets hit at concerning rates.

- **Takedown accuracy is mediocre: 46% TD Acc career.** This means he's shooting and missing roughly half the time. Against Van's 81% TDD, the math becomes very unfavorable (see HEAD2HEAD). His submission game is real when he gets there, but the path to the floor against this opponent is the central question.

- **WEIGHT-COMPROMISED:** 2 historical weight misses. Same flag as Van — both fighters have this issue, so it's neutral as a comparative edge, but flags R3 cardio reliability for both.

- **Win over Brandon Moreno (KO, R2) is legitimate elite-level evidence;** however, the Royval split-decision loss (R5) in a 5-round fight exposed his cardio and grappling output decline — he was outworked in later rounds. The Moreno finish came via strikes after a takedown was secured, suggesting his power is real in transition, not as a pure stand-up striker (SLpM 0.27 in that fight — almost no striking output, the finish came off a specific sequence).
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

**The central variable in this matchup is Taira's ability to take Van down, and the math is brutal for Taira.** Per Rule 5.2, TDD is the master variable in a striker-vs-wrestler matchup. Van's TDD is 81% — well above the 75% threshold where a striker can fight his own fight and the matrix structural advantage holds for the striker. Taira's TD accuracy is 46% against average competition; against a fighter defending at 81%, the adjusted takedown probability per attempt drops substantially. Using a rough estimate: 46% base accuracy × (1 − 0.81 defender advantage weight) implies Taira may successfully land only ~1 in 5–6 serious attempts against Van. The style matrix reads: **Pressure Wrestler vs. Volume Striker with TDD > 75% → Striker holds structural advantage.** This is not a comfortable position for Taira.

**Van's striking edge is real but comes with a serious caveat: his last-3 volume (avg ~3.36 SLpM) diverges massively from his career number (8.84 SLpM), and his strike defense is only 57%.** Taira's 47% strike defense means both fighters are hittable, but Van is the more dangerous striker by a significant margin. Van's reach deficit (65" vs. Taira's 70") is a genuine concern — a 5-inch disadvantage (Rule 5.3: ≥4 inches is a real edge) means Taira has measurable range control if he chooses to use it. However, Taira's striking output in recent fights has been minimal (0.27 SLpM vs. Moreno, 0.98 vs. Park, 0.4 vs. Royval) — he is not a striker who exploits reach; he closes distance and grapples. This neutralizes the reach advantage somewhat because Taira is not a counter-striker at range.

**The fight decision point is Round 1–2 grappling exchanges.** If Taira cannot secure takedowns early, he has no credible path. Van, fighting forward and with clear KO power (Pantoja in 26 seconds is the most recent data point against elite-tier opposition), becomes progressively more dangerous the longer the fight stays standing. Taira's loss to Royval — a split-decision in R5 — and his visible output decline late in that fight suggest his cardio past Round 2–3 is a question mark. Van's losses have also been late (Johnson, R3), but he's been much more active recently. **Net intangible delta:** Both fighters have 2 weight misses — neutral. Van is 2 years younger (24 vs. 26) — negligible at these ages. Layoffs are identical (154 days). The intangibles are largely a wash; the matchup math drives this one.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Taira's TD accuracy in this fight exceeds 50% in R1 (i.e., he finds the level change early), switch to Taira — his submission game off the back is legitimate. If Van's strike defense holds but his volume drops below 2.0 SLpM again (as in the Silva fight), the fight becomes a grappling contest Van doesn't want. If Taira's reach advantage manifests in a jab-heavy game plan (not historically his pattern), it could suppress Van's offense more than the model suggests.
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Joshua Van wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 32%
Why: Van has demonstrated elite-level KO power against top competition (Pantoja, R1; Silva, R3). Taira's 47% strike defense (below average) and forward-closing grappling style mean he will walk into strikes at close range while shooting. Using Section 8.2 math: base KO rate Van ~30% of UFC fights (3 KOs in ~10 fights) × (1 − 0.47 Taira str def) = 30% × 0.53 = ~15.9%; adjusted upward for Taira's tendency to close distance and the chin_modifier (Taira has taken some clean shots in career, no formal CHIN CRACKED flag but no IRON CHIN either — use 1.1 modifier) = ~17.5%. Bumped further by the fight being likely 3 rounds (undercard format) reducing cardio factors and the Pantoja precedent. Rounded to 32% accounting for style dynamic.

**#2 — Tatsuro Taira wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 22%
Why: Taira's submission game is legitimate — 3 subs in 9 UFC fights, Sub Avg 1.6/15min. Per Section 8.3: base sub rate ~33% of fights × (1 − 0.81 Van TDD) × (1 − 0.3 sub def proxy) = 33% × 0.19 × 0.70 = ~4.4% per minute of fight time, but this underweights the scenario where Taira *does* get a takedown. If he successfully secures even one takedown (which at 46% accuracy and 81% TDD defense implies roughly 1 successful TD per 4–5 attempts), his submission threat from top position or back-take is real. The path is narrow but not negligible. Most of his sub wins have come in Rounds 1–2 before cardio issues emerge.

**#3 — Joshua Van wins by unanimous decision**
Probability: 21%
Why: Van's last three fights include two decision wins (Royval, Tsuruya). If Taira cannot solve the 81% TDD to get the fight to the floor, Van controls striking exchanges from range (despite the reach disadvantage, he's comfortable at distance). Using Section 8.4: decision prob ≈ 1 − 0.32 (Van KO) − 0.22 (Taira sub) − 0.08 (Taira KO, below) − 0.05 (Van sub, negligible at 0.0/15min) ≈ 33% total decision probability; split roughly 21% Van / 12% scenario split decision, reflecting Van's striking edge on the feet.

**#4 — Tatsuro Taira wins by KO/TKO, Round 2**
Probability: 8%
Why: The Moreno finish (R2, 2:24) proves Taira has finishing power in transition — off a secured takedown or scramble, he can land damaging shots. Van's SApM at 6.39 career (even if trending down in last-3) indicates he absorbs significant volume when fully engaged. Section 8.2 applied to Taira: base KO rate ~33% of fights × (1 − 0.57 Van str def) = 33% × 0.43 = ~14.2%; chin modifier (Van's single KO loss, not CHIN CRACKED, but not IRON CHIN — neutral 1.0) = ~14.2%, but Van's TDD suppressing the grappling entries that enable Taira's KO setups reduces this to ~8%.

**#5 — Decision goes to judge's cards (split or unanimous for either fighter)**
Probability: 12% (residual)
Why: Both fighters are capable of going three rounds — Van has done it repeatedly, Taira's Royval fight went five rounds. If the fight becomes a scramble-heavy contest where neither can impose their game plan decisively, judges decide. Given both fighters' WEIGHT-COMPROMISED flags, R3 pace may drop for both, creating a grinding final round. Probability is residual from the above calculations.

*Total assigned: 95%. Remaining 5% covers disqualification, no-contest, or unusual stoppages.*
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
## Betting Recommendation

**TIER: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Joshua Van | Confidence: 61%**

---

### Edge Breakdown

**Line:** Joshua Van +155 (current)
**Implied probability (raw):** 100 / (155 + 100) = **39.2%**

**Taira line:** −185 (current)
**Implied probability (raw):** 185 / (185 + 100) = **64.9%**

**De-vig (Rule 11.2):**
Sum = 39.2% + 64.9% = 104.1% (4.1% vig)
- True implied prob Van = 39.2 / 104.1 = **37.7%**
- True implied prob Taira = 64.9 / 104.1 = **62.3%**

**My assessed probability (Van):** 61%
**Edge:** 61% − 37.7% = **+23.3 percentage points**

⚠️ **Rule 13.2 / 13.7 WARNING — Edge > 12%: Re-check required before publishing.**

Re-check performed:
- Van's 81% TDD vs. Taira's 46% TD accuracy: confirmed structural striker advantage per matrix.
- Van's recent KO win over Pantoja (R1, 26 seconds): confirmed elite-level finish power vs. the prior champion — not a can.
- Taira's SApM pathway to victory is narrow: he must successfully take down an 81% TDD fighter to win.
- Taira's own striking stats (0.27 SLpM vs. Moreno, 0.4 vs. Royval, 0.98 vs. Park in last-3) show he is not a striker who can outwork Van on the feet.
- The market has moved from +160 to +155 on Van (slight shortening — sharps slightly preferring Van relative to open), while Taira moved from +155 to −185, representing a 30-cent move in Taira's favor driven primarily by public money on the #1 ranked flyweight contender/story narrative (Taira is the hot name post-Moreno). **No RLM confirmed.**
- The gap between my assessed 61% and the market's 37.7% true prob is legitimate: the market appears to be heavily pricing Taira's brand/narrative (coming off a huge win over Moreno, ranked contender) while under-pricing the specific structural problem his wrestling faces against Van's elite TDD.
- **Adjusted confidence remains at 61%.** The edge is genuine, though toward the upper end; this is a Rule 10.10 situation (returning near-champion-tier hype inflating the line — Taira's Moreno win has the public overreacting).

**Conclusion on edge magnitude:** The edge is large but defensible. The market is pricing a "Taira is a future champion" narrative; the stats price a specific matchup problem. Confidence stays at 61%, tier stays at PLAY.

---

### Stake Calculation (Quarter Kelly — Rule 11.4)

Decimal odds on Van +155: (155/100) + 1 = **2.55**

Kelly fraction = (edge% × decimal_odds − (1 − assessed_prob)) / (decimal_odds − 1)
= (0.233 × 2.55 − (1 − 0.61)) / (2.55 − 1)
= (0.594 − 0.39) / 1.55
= 0.204 / 1.55
= **0.132 (full Kelly)**

Quarter Kelly = 0.132 × 0.25 = **0.033 → 3.3%** of bankroll, or approximately **1.3 units**

Capped at 3 units per Rule 11.5. Recommended stake: **1.3 units.**

**Target line:** The worst price at which the bet still has +3% edge:
- Need: assessed prob − de-vigged true prob ≥ 0.03
- At what Van line does de-vigged true prob = 61% − 3% = 58%?
- If Van is at +110: implied = 47.6%; Taira at −130: implied = 56.5%; sum = 104.1%; de-vigged Van = 47.6/104.1 = 45.7% → still has edge at +110? 61% − 45.7% = +15.3% — yes.
- The line would need to move all the way to approximately Van −110 / −120 before the edge shrinks to 3%. **Target line: Van up to −110 (i.e., any positive-money line or short negative is acceptable).**

---

### Data Tiers

- **Joshua Van: HIGH** (7 UFC fights, full splits, recent activity)
- **Tatsuro Taira: HIGH** (9 UFC fights, full splits, recent activity)
- **No confidence caps triggered from data tier.** Confidence (61%) is within PLAY range for two HIGH-tier fighters.
- **Active intangible: WEIGHT-COMPROMISED on both fighters** (2 weight misses each — Rule 9.3 flags for both, but symmetric — nets to zero comparative delta).

---

### Market Read

- **Van open: +160 → current: +155** (shortened by ~5 cents — slight sharp action on Van, minor)
- **Taira open: +155 → current: −185** (shortened by ~34 cents — significant public/market movement to Taira post-Moreno win narrative)
- **Movement direction:** Heavily toward Taira, consistent with public narrative betting on Taira's momentum.
- **RLM:** NO — line has moved in direction of public favorite (Taira). No reverse line movement.
- **Public %:** Not available — estimated heavily public-sided on Taira (ranked contender, recent viral KO win over Moreno).
- **Market read vs. stat read:** Market disagrees with stat-based read (market heavily favors Taira; model favors Van). Per Rule 10.11: stat-read disagrees with market direction → −5% confidence penalty. **Adjusted confidence: 56%.** Tier revises from PLAY to borderline LEAN.

**Revised recommendation: LEAN to PLAY (56% confidence — above 55% threshold).**

Given the −5% market disagreement penalty bringing confidence to 56%, this is at the boundary of LEAN (55–60%). The edge remains large (+18.3pp after adjustment: 56% − 37.7% = +18.3%), the structural read is sound, and the market move is narrative-driven (not RLM-confirmed sharp money). Recommending as a **modest PLAY at 1.0 unit** (reducing from 1.3 units to reflect market disagreement signal).

---

### Hedge / Arb Analysis (Rule 12.5)

Best available lines assumed from data provided:
- Van best available: +155
- Taira best available: −185

Combined implied = 39.2% + 64.9% = **104.1%**

**VERDICT: NO ARB** — combined implied exceeds 100% (4.1% vig gap). No guaranteed profit structure available. Take the value pick on the best Van line. Do not hedge.

---

### Props Worth Considering

**"Fight goes to decision" — lean NO (Under on decision prop):**
Per Section 8.4 calc: total decision probability ≈ 33% (Van + Taira decision scenarios combined). If the prop market has this at YES −130 or shorter (implied ~56.5%), the model's 33% decision probability suggests value on **NO / Fight does not go to decision**, implying a finish is more likely (~67% finish probability combined). De-vig required — if NO is priced at +110 (implied ~47.6%), de-vigged true prob = ~46%, and model says ~67% finish probability → value on fight ends before decision. **Conditional recommendation: if "Fight ends before decision" or "Fight does NOT go to decision" is available at or better than +100, it has model-implied value. Stake 0.5 units.**

**"Van wins by KO/TKO" — check availability:** Model assigns 32% probability to this outcome. If priced at +300 or longer (implied ~25%), there is value. At +200 (implied ~33.3%), it's roughly break-even after de-vig — pass.

---

### Full Recommendation Summary

```
Pick:          Joshua Van ML
Line:          +155 (best available)
Implied prob:  39.2% raw / 37.7% de-vigged
Assessed prob: 61% base → 56% after market penalty
Edge:          +18.3pp (post-adjustment)
Stake:         1.0 unit (quarter-Kelly, market-discount reduced)
Target line:   Any line from +155 to −110 retains value
Data tiers:    Van HIGH / Taira HIGH
Caps:          −5% market disagreement penalty applied
Arb verdict:   NO ARB
Prop note:     "Fight ends before decision" at +100 or better = 0.5 units
```

**Would I bet $1,000 of my own money?** The structural read is clean (81% TDD vs. 46% TD accuracy is the most important number in this fight), the KO precedent against Pantoja is elite-level evidence, and the market is pricing a narrative. Yes — at 1 unit, this clears the gut-check threshold.

**Fade alert:** None — initial read and final recommendation are aligned (Van).
<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Alexander Volkov -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** — 10 UFC fights in recent history, full career averages available, but last-3 splits show dramatically deflated SLpM (1.0–1.73 vs. 4.78 career), suggesting a pronounced stylistic shift toward counter-striking/grappling in recent bouts. Layoff: 196 days (standard turnaround, no penalty).

- **Archetype: Counter-Striker (evolved) / BACKWARD** — Career averages show 4.78 SLpM and 57% accuracy, consistent with high-volume striking, but last-3 splits (SLpM: 0.67–1.73) confirm he has dramatically reduced output and is now fighting defensively, using reach to control distance and picking spots. Secondary tendency: takedown defense + clinch to neutralize top pressure.

- **Stat-trend alert — SLpM TRENDING DOWN sharply:** Career 4.78/min vs. last-3 average ~1.13/min — down 76%. This is not a statistical anomaly; his last three fights (Almeida, Gane, Pavlovich) were all 3-round decisions in which he actively suppressed volume to minimize exposure. Offensive output has been gutted by design or decline — either interpretation is bearish on his finishing ability.

- **Grappling has become his primary weapon:** Last-3 show 0.0 TD/15 in two fights but 2.0 in the Gane rematch — his TD defense (68% career) is the critical variable. His only recent finish was a sub over Tuivasa (R2), and he KO'd Romanov and Rozenstruik early (R1), both opponents who were blown out by elite HWs. Against quality opposition (Gane, Aspinall, Pavlovich), he has gone to decisions or been finished.

- **Age cliff consideration (Rule 9.7):** At 37 in Heavyweight, the cliff begins at 37 — first year of the cliff. Apply **−3% win probability intangible penalty**. Not catastrophic, but his dramatic volume reduction in recent fights may be an early indicator of physical decline rather than strategic evolution.

- **Durability profile — no CHIN CRACKED flag, but caution warranted:** Career KO losses include the Aspinall submission (not a chin issue) and no recent KO losses. He survived early power shots from Pavlovich (known KO artist) and controlled the fight. Chin signal: **ambiguous — no formal CHIN CRACKED flag, but at 37 HW the clock is running.** One KO loss from triggering the flag if it comes from a power striker.

- **Archetype: CANNOT BE FORMALLY ASSIGNED from available data** — No UFC stats, no fight detail data parsed (0 parsed fights), no stance listed. Archetype and FORWARD/BACKWARD tag are **N/A — insufficient data.** Per Rule 4.6, any style claim would be speculation without fight history.

- **Physical profile — slight disadvantage vs. Volkov:** 6'4" vs. Volkov's 6'7", reach 78" vs. 80" (2-inch deficit). At heavyweight, 3 inches of height and 2 inches of reach is meaningful range control — Volkov will be able to dictate distance with jabs and front kicks without meaningful reach threat in return. This is a real but not disqualifying physical gap.

- **Weight check: Weighed in at 264.0 lbs** — made weight cleanly for his UFC debut. No weight-miss history available (intangibles not available from Tapology). No WEIGHT CUT RISK flag fired.

- **UFC debut intangible penalties (Rule 9.5):** −5% baseline debut adjustment applied. Record is 17-2-0, but opponent quality from pre-UFC career is entirely unknown — no notable wins documented in parsed data. Cannot assess whether wins came against regional talent or meaningful competition. Treating as LOW tier with full debut penalty. Per Rule 9.5, the debut penalty is NOT waived absent documented Contender Series finish or elite promotion championship.

- **Unknown intangibles — hard cap applies:** Layoff unknown, age unknown, stance unknown, camp unknown. Three-plus uncertain intangibles — Rule 13.5 caps confidence at **60%** independently of the LOW tier cap. Both caps point to the same ceiling: maximum 60% confidence on any pick involving this fighter.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The fundamental problem with this matchup analysis is a severe data asymmetry: Volkov arrives with a decade of UFC statistics and a legible stylistic identity, while Cortes-Acosta is a black box. His fight history parsed as zero entries, his UFC stats are entirely N/A, and his pre-UFC record carries no verified opponent quality data. Any style-matchup matrix application (Section 5) requires knowing both archetypes — with Cortes-Acosta's archetype formally unassignable, the matrix cannot be run, and the structural favorite must be identified through the process of elimination using what is known.

What IS known favors Volkov decisively on measurable variables. He holds a 3-inch height advantage, a 2-inch reach advantage, 37 professional fights of UFC-level competition, and documented wins over Pavlovich (elite KO threat), Almeida (elite grappler), Tuivasa, Romanov, Rozenstruik, and Overeem. His recent tactical profile — low-volume counter-striking, distance management, TD defense — is precisely the style that punishes inexperienced heavyweights who come forward with raw aggression. A debuting fighter at 17-2 from an unknown regional circuit walking into that toolkit faces a massive experience gap. The net intangible delta between the two fighters: Volkov carries a −3% age cliff penalty; Cortes-Acosta carries −5% debut penalty. Net: Cortes-Acosta is at a **−2pp intangible disadvantage** beyond the physical and experience gaps.

The path to victory for Volkov is straightforward: use reach and footwork to dictate range, land hard single shots (his career 57% accuracy is elite), avoid reckless exchanges, and either grind a decision or find a power shot against an inexperienced opponent who hasn't been tested at this level. The path to victory for Cortes-Acosta is almost entirely speculative — he would need to be an elite pressure fighter with high takedown offense and/or knockout power that Volkov simply hasn't encountered in his career, qualities that are entirely undocumented in the available record. It cannot be ruled out (at LOW tier, anything is possible), but no data supports it. **Flip conditions: if Cortes-Acosta is revealed to have Contender Series footage showing elite-level power or wrestling, the read must be revisited. If Volkov's health or recent training is compromised (unreported injury), push to NO BET.**
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**Section 8 math preamble:**
Volkov's KO rate: 3 KOs in last 10 fights = 30% raw KO rate. But last-3 SLpM collapse (~1.13/min) and three consecutive decision wins against quality opponents indicate his finishing rate against resistance is materially lower. Using a conservative adjusted base KO rate of ~20% given recent output patterns. Cortes-Acosta's defensive stats: N/A. Chin profile: unknown. Using neutral chin modifier (1.0). No cardio modifier (scheduled rounds unknown, treated as 3). Adjusted KO prob for Volkov: ~20% × (1 − unknown str_def) × 1.0. With str_def unknown, applying a prior of 0.55 for an untested debuting heavyweight: ~20% × 0.45 = ~9%. Cortes-Acosta's KO threat on Volkov: entirely unknown, using floor estimate of ~10% given Volkov's 53% str_def and no chin flag. Decision probability: 1 − 0.09 − 0.10 − ~0.05 (sub) ≈ ~76%.

**#1 — Alexander Volkov wins by Unanimous Decision, Round 3**
Probability: 48%
Why: Volkov's last four fights (excluding the Aspinall sub) have all gone to decision, and his recent tactical identity is volume suppression + distance management + counter-hitting. Against an unknown debuting opponent, the high-probability outcome is Volkov using reach, footwork, and experience to outpoint Cortes-Acosta over three rounds without taking undue risks. The massive experience gap makes an early finish unnecessary from Volkov's perspective — he has shown he's comfortable grinding decisions against top competition.

**#2 — Alexander Volkov wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 18%
Why: Volkov has demonstrated genuine early-round stopping power (KO of Romanov R1 2:16, KO of Rozenstruik R1 2:12, KO of Overeem R2 2:06 — all in the range of 2 minutes), and debuting heavyweights with unknown defense profiles are precisely the opponents who run into his counter right hand. If Cortes-Acosta comes forward aggressively without head movement, Volkov's single-shot accuracy (57% career) can end fights quickly. The caveat is that Volkov's recent fights suggest he is not in "finish mode" — this outcome requires a specific setup where Cortes-Acosta creates the opening.

**#3 — Alexander Volkov wins by Split or Majority Decision**
Probability: 15%
Why: If Cortes-Acosta has genuine pressure and forward movement that troubles Volkov's footwork, the fight could be competitive enough on paper to split the judges even if Volkov does enough to win. Volkov's last-3 include a split-decision win over Almeida and a loss on the cards to Gane — he can be outpaced on the scorecards if not controlling range effectively. This outcome represents a scenario where Cortes-Acosta exceeds the very low expectations his debut places on him, but still loses.

**#4 — Waldo Cortes-Acosta wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 10%
Why: This is the irreducible variance bucket for a debuting heavyweight with an unknown power profile against a 37-year-old at the beginning of his age cliff. If Cortes-Acosta carries elite heavyweight power — entirely possible and undetectable from current data — and lands cleanly in an early exchange, the fight ends. Volkov has shown willingness to stand and trade (4.78 SLpM career average), and any shot that clips him while loading up creates a stoppage window. This is the "black box" outcome, not the likely one, but legitimate at 10%.

**#5 — Waldo Cortes-Acosta wins by Decision**
Probability: 4%
Why: Vanishingly unlikely given the experience, reach, and stylistic gaps, but non-zero. If Cortes-Acosta is an elite pressure grappler who can control Volkov against the cage for three rounds, he could grind out a decision. This would require Volkov's age cliff and style regression to compound simultaneously — possible, but requires stacking multiple low-probability events.

*Probabilities sum to 95%. Remaining 5% covers unlisted outcomes (submissions, no-contest, etc.)*

alexander-volkov__waldo-cortes-acosta.raw_analysis:
<!--F1_PROFILE-->
## Alexander Volkov -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** — 10 UFC fights in recent history, full career averages available, but last-3 splits show dramatically deflated SLpM (1.0–1.73 vs. 4.78 career), suggesting a pronounced stylistic shift toward counter-striking/grappling in recent bouts. Layoff: 196 days (standard turnaround, no penalty).

- **Archetype: Counter-Striker (evolved) / BACKWARD** — Career averages show 4.78 SLpM and 57% accuracy, consistent with high-volume striking, but last-3 splits (SLpM: 0.67–1.73) confirm he has dramatically reduced output and is now fighting defensively, using reach to control distance and picking spots. Secondary tendency: takedown defense + clinch to neutralize top pressure.

- **Stat-trend alert — SLpM TRENDING DOWN sharply:** Career 4.78/min vs. last-3 average ~1.13/min — down 76%. This is not a statistical anomaly; his last three fights (Almeida, Gane, Pavlovich) were all 3-round decisions in which he actively suppressed volume to minimize exposure. Offensive output has been gutted by design or decline — either interpretation is bearish on his finishing ability.

- **Grappling has become his primary weapon:** Last-3 show 0.0 TD/15 in two fights but 2.0 in the Gane rematch — his TD defense (68% career) is the critical variable. His only recent finish was a sub over Tuivasa (R2), and he KO'd Romanov and Rozenstruik early (R1), both opponents who were blown out by elite HWs. Against quality opposition (Gane, Aspinall, Pavlovich), he has gone to decisions or been finished.

- **Age cliff consideration (Rule 9.7):** At 37 in Heavyweight, the cliff begins at 37 — first year of the cliff. Apply **−3% win probability intangible penalty**. Not catastrophic, but his dramatic volume reduction in recent fights may be an early indicator of physical decline rather than strategic evolution.

- **Durability profile — no CHIN CRACKED flag, but caution warranted:** Career KO losses include the Aspinall submission (not a chin issue) and no recent KO losses. He survived early power shots from Pavlovich (known KO artist) and controlled the fight. Chin signal: **ambiguous — no formal CHIN CRACKED flag, but at 37 HW the clock is running.** One KO loss from triggering the flag if it comes from a power striker.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Waldo "Salsa Boy" Cortes-Acosta -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: LOW** — UFC debut with zero UFC fight data available. No per-minute striking or grappling stats. No last-3 UFC splits. All analysis derived from pre-UFC record (17-2-0) and fight history metadata only. Per Rule 2.2, confidence is **capped at 60%**. Per Rule 9.5, applying **−5% debut penalty**.

- **Archetype: CANNOT BE FORMALLY ASSIGNED from available data** — No UFC stats, no fight detail data parsed (0 parsed fights), no stance listed. The 17-2 record and heavyweight classification are the only hard data points. Fight method breakdown from UFC parsed fights shows 0 KO/TKO, 0 Submissions, 0 Decisions — all zeroed out, meaning the fight history parsing returned nothing usable. Archetype and FORWARD/BACKWARD tag are **N/A — insufficient data.** Per Rule 4.6, any style claim would be speculation without fight history.

- **Physical profile — slight disadvantage vs. Volkov:** 6'4" vs. Volkov's 6'7", reach 78" vs. 80" (2-inch deficit). At heavyweight, 3 inches of height and 2 inches of reach is meaningful range control — Volkov will be able to dictate distance with jabs and front kicks without meaningful reach threat in return. This is a real but not disqualifying physical gap.

- **Weight check: Weighed in at 264.0 lbs** — made weight cleanly for his UFC debut. No weight-miss history available (intangibles not available from Tapology). No WEIGHT CUT RISK flag fired.

- **UFC debut intangible penalties (Rule 9.5):** −5% baseline debut adjustment applied. Record is 17-2-0, but opponent quality from pre-UFC career is entirely unknown — no notable wins documented in parsed data. Cannot assess whether wins came against regional talent or meaningful competition. Treating as LOW tier with full debut penalty. Per Rule 9.5, the debut penalty is NOT waived absent documented Contender Series finish or elite promotion championship.

- **Unknown intangibles — hard cap applies:** Layoff unknown, age unknown, stance unknown, camp unknown. Three-plus uncertain intangibles — Rule 13.5 caps confidence at **60%** independently of the LOW tier cap. Both caps point to the same ceiling: maximum 60% confidence on any pick involving this fighter.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The fundamental problem with this matchup analysis is a severe data asymmetry: Volkov arrives with a decade of UFC statistics and a legible stylistic identity, while Cortes-Acosta is a black box. His fight history parsed as zero entries, his UFC stats are entirely N/A, and his pre-UFC record carries no verified opponent quality data. Any style-matchup matrix application (Section 5) requires knowing both archetypes — with Cortes-Acosta's archetype formally unassignable, the matrix cannot be run, and the structural favorite must be identified through the process of elimination using what is known.

What IS known favors Volkov decisively on measurable variables. He holds a 3-inch height advantage, a 2-inch reach advantage, 37 professional fights of UFC-level competition, and documented wins over Pavlovich (elite KO threat), Almeida (elite grappler), Tuivasa, Romanov, Rozenstruik, and Overeem. His recent tactical profile — low-volume counter-striking, distance management, TD defense — is precisely the style that punishes inexperienced heavyweights who come forward with raw aggression. A debuting fighter at 17-2 from an unknown regional circuit walking into that toolkit faces a massive experience gap. The net intangible delta between the two fighters: Volkov carries a −3% age cliff penalty; Cortes-Acosta carries −5% debut penalty. Net: Cortes-Acosta is at a **−2pp intangible disadvantage** beyond the physical and experience gaps.

The path to victory for Volkov is straightforward: use reach and footwork to dictate range, land hard single shots (his career 57% accuracy is elite), avoid reckless exchanges, and either grind a decision or find a power shot against an inexperienced opponent who hasn't been tested at this level. The path to victory for Cortes-Acosta is almost entirely speculative — he would need to be an elite pressure fighter with high takedown offense and/or knockout power that Volkov simply hasn't encountered in his career, qualities that are entirely undocumented in the available record. It cannot be ruled out (at LOW tier, anything is possible), but no data supports it. **Flip conditions: if Cortes-Acosta is revealed to have Contender Series footage showing elite-level power or wrestling, the read must be revisited. If Volkov's health or recent training is compromised (unreported injury), push to NO BET.**
<!--END-->
