- **100% TD defense** (career, UFC sample) is a meaningful marker even on small sample — he has not been taken down in his UFC run, which matters against any opponent who might panic-shoot.
- **Finishing machine**: 2 KO/TKOs and 1 submission in last 3 fights, all inside distance. Fights average under 2 rounds. No decisions in UFC career (11-0 overall, 0 decisions). This is not a fighter who drags fights into the championship rounds.
- **Reach advantage: +4 inches** (79" vs. 75") — a real structural edge (Rule 5.3 threshold: ≥4 inches). Allows him to dictate range as a forward-pressure fighter while keeping Santos at the end of his punches.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Djorden Santos -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** (3 UFC fights, full career averages available). Archetype: **Volume Striker (degraded)** — career 7.00 SLpM is elite volume, but last-3 splits (1.80, 1.67, 1.40 SLpM avg ~1.62/min) represent a **catastrophic stat-trend collapse**. Direction tag: **FORWARD** by archetype, but last-3 evidence suggests passive/reactive execution.
- **CRITICAL STAT-TREND ALERT — SLpM TRENDING DOWN (SEVERE)**: Last-3 avg SLpM ~1.62 vs. career 7.00 — TRENDING DOWN (−77%). This is not noise; it is a fundamental behavioral shift. Santos is not fighting like a volume striker in the UFC. Three consecutive fights with sub-2.0 SLpM suggests either significant style adjustment, opponent-driven suppression, or camp issues. The career avg is essentially fiction at this point.
- **Strike defense is poor and worsening**: Career SApM 5.98 absorbed/min with only 48% strike defense. Against a precision striker like Susurkaev (59% accuracy), this is a severe liability. Even at Susurkaev's suppressed last-3 volume, his accuracy means Santos absorbs meaningful shots per exchange.
- **Grappling is an afterthought**: 0.67 TD avg, 0.0 sub attempts/15min, and last-3 splits show 0 takedowns across all three fights. TD defense of 86% is a positive marker but he will not initiate wrestling — he is purely a striker who cannot use the floor as a safety valve.
- **Last-3 fight record: 2-1, all decisions** — but the wins are unconvincing. vs. Danny Barlow (WIN): 1.80 SLpM, 55% accuracy — modest. vs. Will Currie (WIN): 1.40 SLpM, 47.7% accuracy — passive. vs. Ozzy Diaz (LOSS): 1.67 SLpM, 35.7% accuracy — limp. Santos is not finishing anyone and is barely out-working journeyman-level opponents.
- **No CHIN CRACKED flag** (only 2 losses in career, no KO losses confirmed in fight history data). However, career SApM of 5.98 means he absorbs significant volume when fights open up — a long-term accumulation risk that matters against a finisher.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The style-matchup matrix gives a clear structural read: **Power/Pressure Striker (Susurkaev) vs. Volume Striker (Santos, degraded)**. Per the matrix, a power striker beats a volume striker early, and volume wins late — but Santos's last-3 data has stripped him of the "volume" identity entirely. His last-3 SLpM of ~1.62/min is not a volume fighter; it's a passive decision-maker. That reframing flips the matrix outcome even further in Susurkaev's favor. The structural question becomes: can Santos survive long enough to implement any gameplan, and does he even have one that works here?

The critical exchange in this fight is **striking accuracy vs. absorption rate**. Susurkaev lands 59% of his strikes. Santos absorbs at a career rate of 5.98 SLpM with only 48% defense. Even at Susurkaev's suppressed last-3 pace (~2.36 SLpM), his precision means nearly every exchange generates meaningful damage on Santos. The +4 inch reach advantage compounds this — Susurkaev can initiate from a range where Santos's counters (if they come) fall short, and Santos will be forced to either close the distance (walking into shots) or fight on the outside at a range disadvantage. Santos's TDD of 86% is good enough to prevent a wrestling-only gameplan from dominating, but Susurkaev's 100% TDD means Santos cannot use a reactive takedown to escape trouble either.

The net intangible delta is essentially neutral: Susurkaev is 175 days out (within standard turnaround, no penalty), Santos 203 days out (mild ring rust threshold at 180 days, −3%). Both are relatively young (25 vs. 28). No camp change or weight-miss data available. Susurkaev's age and 11-0 record with 100% finish rate in the UFC represent the only meaningful intangible edge. Santos's path to winning requires him to rediscover the 7.00 SLpM fighter he was on paper — a fighter who has not shown up in three consecutive UFC bouts — and outlast a finisher who has never been taken the distance professionally. That is a significant ask against a physically longer, more accurate, and demonstrably more dangerous opponent.

**Flip conditions (Rule 15.1):** If Santos's career SLpM of 7.00 represents his true baseline and last-3 was opponent-suppressed (high-pace opponents forcing conservative positioning), the volume gap closes — but Susurkaev's accuracy and reach still win the exchange. If Susurkaev's 100% TDD cracks on a first real test from a competent grappler — Santos is not that test (0 TDs in last 3). If Susurkaev's low last-3 SLpM reflects a physical issue (injury camp), his finishing threat diminishes — but he still has the reach, accuracy, and grappling floor. **None of these flip conditions are supported by current data.**
<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Baisangur Susurkaev wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1–2**
Probability: 52%
Why: Susurkaev has finished 3 of his last 3 UFC opponents, two by KO. Santos absorbs at a 5.98 SLpM career rate with 48% defense against a 59%-accurate striker with a 4-inch reach advantage. Using the Section 8 KO adjustment: base KO rate ~40% of fights (conservative, given sub finishes too), × (1 − 0.48 defense) = 0.40 × 0.52 × 1.0 chin modifier × 1.0 cardio = ~20.8% per exchange window — but Susurkaev's accuracy and pressure compound across rounds, and fight history shows he closes before R3. Santos has shown no ability to neutralize pressure finishers in his UFC run.

**#2 — Baisangur Susurkaev wins by Submission, Round 1–2**
Probability: 18%
Why: Susurkaev has 0.7 sub attempts/15min and submitted Eric Nolan in his last three fights. If the striking creates urgency and Santos shoots or clinches to survive, Susurkaev's 100% TDD and active submission game becomes a threat. Santos has 0 submission losses in his career and 86% TDD, so the path requires Santos to voluntarily enter a clinch/scramble situation — plausible if he's hurt standing.

**#3 — Baisangur Susurkaev wins by KO/TKO or TKO, Round 3**
Probability: 14%
Why: Santos has survived three consecutive UFC fights to the final bell, suggesting durability at low-paced fights. If Susurkaev is patient (as shown by the McConico R3 finish), the finish may come later. Still a Susurkaev finish — just at the later round timeline. Santos's 48% defense eventually breaks against continued precision pressure.

**#4 — Baisangur Susurkaev wins by Decision**
Probability: 8%
Why: Section 8.4 decision probability check: 1 − (0.52 + 0.18 + 0.14) − sub_Santos(~2%) ≈ 14% total non-finish, but Susurkaev has 0 career decisions. The only path here is Santos surviving on movement and Susurkaev being unable to close — inconsistent with his fight history and Santos's passive offense providing no disincentive to finish-seeking.

**#5 — Djorden Santos wins by Decision**
Probability: 5%
Why: Santos's only win condition is outworking Susurkaev on volume (his career 7.00 SLpM vs. Susurkaev's last-3 ~2.36) across three full rounds, while Susurkaev misses takedowns and cannot close. Requires Santos to fight like his career average and Susurkaev to fight like his suppressed last-3 — both simultaneously. The probability is non-zero but requires two unlikely concurrent events.
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
## Betting Recommendation

**1. Tier Label: NO BET (on the moneyline)**

**2. Primary Pick: PASS on Susurkaev moneyline**
Confidence: 91% Susurkaev wins — but confidence without edge is not a bet.

**3. Edge Breakdown (Moneyline):**
- Line: Susurkaev −800 (current)
- Implied probability: 800 / (800 + 100) = **88.9%**
- De-vig: Susurkaev −800 / Santos +375
  - Implied F1: 88.9%, Implied F2: 100/475 = 21.1%. Sum = 110.0% (10.0% vig — very high, likely book inefficiency/lopsided action)
  - De-vigged true F1: 88.9 / 110.0 = **80.8%**
  - De-vigged true F2: 21.1 / 110.0 = **19.2%**
- Assessed probability (Susurkaev): **91%** (style mismatch + finish rate + reach + Santos stat collapse)
- Edge: 91% − 80.8% = **+10.2%**

*Normally +10.2% edge would be a STRONG PLAY. But at −800, the quarter-Kelly math collapses the stake to nearly zero and the variance makes this unplayable as a moneyline.*

Quarter-Kelly math:
- Decimal odds for −800: (100/800) + 1 = 1.125
- Kelly fraction = (0.102 × 1.125 − (1 − 0.91)) / (1.125 − 1) = (0.1148 − 0.09) / 0.125 = 0.0248 / 0.125 = **0.198**
- Quarter Kelly = 0.198 × 0.25 = **0.05 units**

*0.05 units on a −800 favorite. This is mathematically valid but practically worthless — risk $800 to profit $100 with 5 cents staked. The edge exists; the line makes it unplayable as a moneyline.*

**Target line:** The worst price at which +3% edge survives is approximately Susurkaev −**550** (implied 84.6%, de-vigged ~81.2% at typical vig — assessed 91% still gives 9.8% edge). The current −800 line has passed this target by 250 cents. **Value is gone on the moneyline.**

**4. Stake Recommendation: 0.05 units (moneyline — DO NOT PLACE. Round to 0.)**

**5. Target Line:** Susurkaev −550 or better. Current market is −800. **Value gone on the moneyline.**

**6. Data Tiers:**
- Susurkaev: **MEDIUM** (3 UFC fights, complete but small sample)
- Santos: **MEDIUM** (3 UFC fights, complete)
- Confidence cap: 67% maximum per Rule 13.4 (both MEDIUM). However, assessed prob of 91% is a *win probability estimate*, not a betting confidence claim. Betting confidence on any play here is capped at 67% per data tier rules — reinforcing the NO BET on moneyline (the edge math already kills it at −800 regardless).

**7. Market Read:**
- Open: Susurkaev −500 / Santos +700 (implied F1 83.3% / F2 12.5%, de-vigged ~87%/13%)
- Current: Susurkaev −800 / Santos +375
- Movement: **300 cents toward Susurkaev** — the line has shortened dramatically
- Direction: Strong public/sharp money piling onto Susurkaev, driving the line from −500 to −800
- RLM: **NO** — line moved in the direction of Susurkaev betting, consistent with public action. No reverse line movement signal.
- Sharp signal: The movement is large but directionally consistent with public favorites. No RLM means no sharp-vs-public divergence to exploit. The stat read agrees with the market direction — Susurkaev is the correct favorite — but the line has been bid past the value point.
- Market read **agrees** with stat-based read on direction; disagrees on magnitude (market has overshot the value point).

**8. Hedge Verdict:**
- Best F1 line: Susurkaev −800 → implied 88.9%
- Best F2 line: Santos +375 → implied 21.1%
- Combined implied: 88.9% + 21.1% = **110.0%**
- **NO ARB** — combined implied is 110.0%, well above 100%. No arbitrage opportunity. Take the value pick on the best available line if there is one — but as established, the moneyline value is gone.

**9. Props Worth Considering:**

*Fight does NOT go to decision (Susurkaev finishes inside distance):*
- Susurkaev has 0 career decisions (11-0, 100% finish rate). Santos has 0 KO losses but 48% defense against a 59%-accurate finisher. Calculated decision probability from Section 8.4: ~8% (Endings section). If the "fight goes to decision" prop is priced at YES −200 or longer, that implies ~66.7% decision probability — wildly inconsistent with the ~8% model estimate. This is the **primary value prop.**
- If the prop market offers "fight ends in R1 or R2" YES at reasonable odds: model assigns 70% probability to a Rounds 1–2 finish (52% KO + 18% sub). If priced at YES −150 (implied 60%), that's a **+10% edge** — a genuine value play.
- **De-vig check on any "over 1.5 rounds" NO prop**: If available, model assigns 70% to a finish in R1–R2, meaning "fight ends before 1.5 rounds" YES at −150 (implied 60%) = +10% edge. Require 5%+ edge for props per Rule 11.7 — **this clears the bar.** Seek this prop.

*Recommended prop play (if available):*
- **"Fight ends in Round 1 or Round 2" YES** — target price: YES −175 or better (implied 63.6%, assessed 70%, edge +6.4%). **PLAY at up to 1.5 units.**
- **"Fight goes to decision" NO** — same underlying thesis, confirm de-vigged pricing before placing.

**10. Fade Alert:**
- No fade alert. The stat-based read and market direction are aligned (Susurkaev wins). The only fade opportunity would be Santos at +375, but the assessed probability of Santos winning (~9%) does not support it (implied de-vigged 19.2% — Santos is still overpriced at +375 relative to his actual ~9% win probability).

---

**SUMMARY:**
- Susurkaev moneyline at −800: **NO BET — value gone** (line has been bid 250 cents past the target of −550).
- Props on early finish (R1–R2 YES or fight goes to decision NO): **PLAY if available at stated price thresholds** — this is where the real value lives in this matchup.
- Susurkaev wins this fight at ~91% probability. The market knows it. Bet the *how*, not the *who*.
<!--END-->

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Clayton Carpenter -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** — 5 UFC fights, full per-fight splits available for last 3, but last-3 sample shows severe inconsistency suggesting data quality issues. Layoff: 210 days (standard turnaround, no penalty).
- **Archetype: Scrambler / Sub Specialist (FORWARD)** — Career Sub Avg. 1.3/15min, 2 of last 3 UFC wins by submission. Builds pressure and hunts for scrambles; secondary mode is decision grinding when takedown entries fail (1 UFC decision win vs. Edgar Chairez).
- **Striking is a volume liability, not a weapon** — Career SLpM 3.66 but last-3 average collapses dramatically: 1.49 (Filho), 1.20 (Ulanbekov), 3.19 (Rocha) — effectively 2.0 last-3 vs. 3.66 career (**TRENDING DOWN −45%**, opponent-quality-adjusted: Filho and Ulanbekov are ranked/top-level competition, making the downtrend high-signal, not noise). Str. Acc. 53% career but only 33.3% vs. Filho — accuracy oscillates wildly, not a reliable weapon.
- **Grappling is also fading** — TD Avg. 1.99/15min career but registered 0.0 TD/15 in each of his last two fights (vs. Filho and Ulanbekov). Only one fight in last 3 showed takedown activity (2.08 vs. Rocha, a win). TD defense at 37% is a critical weakness — he routinely gets taken down and can't get up.
- **TD Defense is the matchup's critical flaw** — 37% career TD Def. is well below the 60% threshold; any opponent with a wrestling game exploits this. However, Ochoa has only 0.43 TD/15min career, so this may not be the decisive vector here.
- **Recent form: 2-fight losing streak** — Lost by sub (R1, 4:42 to Filho) and by unanimous decision to Ulanbekov. Pattern: when his submission game is neutralized by elite grapplers or his pressure is managed, he has no reliable fallback striking threat. No KO wins in last 5 fights.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Jose Ochoa -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** — Only 3 UFC fights in the splits; 1 KO win, 2 decision losses. Small sample but all 3 fights are recent (Nov 2024–Jul 2025). Layoff: 287 days (180–270 day range: −3% intangible penalty applied). **SOUTHPAW.**
- **Archetype: Counter-Striker / Pressure Striker hybrid (BACKWARD-to-FORWARD switch)** — Career SLpM 3.01, Str. Def. 60%, SApM only 2.33. Fights defensively in most appearances (low output, high defense) but showed explosive offense in the Durden KO (4.82 SLpM). Inconsistency in output suggests a fighter who is dangerous in bursts but not sustained pressure.
- **Striking wildly inconsistent across fights** — SLpM: 4.82 (Durden), 1.27 (Kavanagh), 0.67 (Almabayev). This 4.82-to-0.67 swing is not a trend — it is fight-to-fight variability that makes him genuinely difficult to profile. Career Str. Acc. 37% is below average; he finds his mark less than expected. Last-3 acc: 34.2% (Durden), 43.2% (Kavanagh), 55.6% (Almabayev) — accuracy improving when output falls, suggesting he becomes more selective under resistance.
- **KO threat is real but context-dependent** — The Durden KO came in R2 at 0:11 — an extremely fast finish suggesting Durden walked into something early. Win methods last 3: 1 KO, 0 subs, 0 decisions. Base KO rate: 1/3 UFC fights = 33%; small sample, use with caution. Career finish rate is higher pre-UFC but data quality is LOW for pre-UFC opponents.
- **Grappling is minimal** — TD Avg. 0.43/15min career, 50% TD Acc. (small sample), Sub Avg. 1.3/15min but zero submission finishes. He showed 1.0 TD/15 vs. Almabayev — a notable uptick in his most recent fight but still a small sample. **TD Defense 59%** — coin-flip against a grappler, below the 60% threshold.
- **Layoff penalty: −3%** (287 days, 270–365 day bracket per Rule 9.2). No weight miss, camp change, or short-notice flags available (Tapology data N/A).
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The stylistic matrix here is a **Counter-Striker/Pressure-hybrid (Ochoa) vs. Scrambler/Sub Specialist (Carpenter)**. Per the Section 5 matrix, the sub specialist's path to victory requires closing distance and securing scrambles — which in turn requires either a takedown or a clinch entry. Ochoa's TD defense sits at 59%, just below the 60% coin-flip threshold, meaning Carpenter *can* get the fight down, but it is not reliable. Critically, Carpenter registered zero takedowns in each of his last two fights against elite-level opposition, making his grappling entry more theoretical than demonstrated in current form. If Ochoa can keep the fight standing and manage the clinch, he is the structural favorite.

Ochoa's striking advantage is real but fragile. His 60% strike defense vs. Carpenter's career SLpM of 3.66 (current form trending down to ~2.0) means he absorbs fewer meaningful shots when on form. However, Ochoa's own accuracy at 37% career and his output oscillating between 0.67 and 4.82 SLpM across three fights makes him an unreliable striker. The southpaw angle matters here — Carpenter is orthodox and, per Section 5.4, we cannot confirm he has faced southpaws recently (fight history lists no obvious southpaw opponents), so the stance edge is real. Ochoa's left hand lands on the open side against orthodox fighters, and his KO of Durden (orthodox) suggests he can exploit the angle. Net intangible delta: Ochoa −3% (layoff penalty); Carpenter neutral on intangibles (Tapology data N/A, no confirmed penalties). Slight intangible edge to Carpenter by default, but insufficient to override the stylistic read.

**Path to victory — Carpenter:** Gets the fight to the mat through pressure clinch work, secures a dominant scramble, and finishes via submission as he has done 4 times in his career. His 1.3 Sub Avg./15min and multiple UFC submission wins show he can finish from top or guard positions. He needs to land a takedown entry early before Ochoa's range management sets in. **Path to victory — Ochoa:** Keeps the fight standing, uses his southpaw stance and 1-inch reach advantage to land on the open side against Carpenter's orthodox guard, disrupts Carpenter's clinch entries with footwork, and either wins a clear striking decision or finds the right moment for a KO — as he showed against Durden. His 60% strike defense should limit Carpenter's volume impact on the feet.

**Flip conditions:** If Carpenter's takedown attempts materialize at closer to career average (2.0/15min) rather than his recent 0.0/15min, the grappling leverage shifts and Carpenter becomes a live favorite. If Ochoa's output stays near his 0.67 SLpM floor (as seen vs. Almabayev), he may lose a decision rather than finish — opening the door for Carpenter's grinding style.
analysis_sections.endings:
## 3-5 Most Likely Fight Endings (Ranked by Probability)

**#1 — Jose Ochoa wins by decision (unanimous or split), Round 3**
Probability: 32%
Why: Ochoa's 60% strike defense and southpaw angles are the dominant variables here. If he manages range effectively — as he did vs. Almabayev (0.67 SLpM output but only 2.33 SApM absorbed career) — he wins a low-output striking decision. Carpenter's last-3 shows he cannot sustain volume against disciplined opposition (1.20–1.49 SLpM vs. Ulanbekov and Filho), and his takedown rate against top opponents has dropped to zero. Decision probability calculation: adjusted KO prob Ochoa (see below) ~14%, adjusted sub prob Carpenter ~12%, adjusted KO prob Carpenter ~5%, adjusted sub prob Ochoa ~5% → decision probability residual ≈ 64% total, but split between both fighters; Ochoa decision share ~32%.

**#2 — Clayton Carpenter wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 22%
Why: Carpenter's 4 career submission finishes and 1.3 Sub Avg./15min are real threats when he gets the fight to the mat. Adjusted sub probability: base sub rate = 4 subs / 8 total fights = 50%; adjusted for Ochoa's TD defense (59%) → 50% × (1 − 0.59) = 20.5%; further adjusted for Ochoa's sub defense proxy (0 sub losses / 2 total losses = 1.0 resistance proxy → sub_def_proxy = 0.0, using default 0.7) → 20.5% × (1 − 0.7) = 6.2% pure sub probability from ground. However, Carpenter's last-3 entry success is 0.0 TD/15min vs. elite opposition, and Ochoa is not elite — his 59% TDD gives Carpenter more realistic entry chances here than in prior fights. Upward adjustment to ~22% given the weaker opposition quality and Carpenter's sub finishing ability.

**#3 — Jose Ochoa wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Probability: 18%
Why: Ochoa's KO of Durden shows he has one-shot power in his southpaw arsenal. Adjusted KO probability: base KO rate = 1/3 UFC fights = 33% (small sample, discount to ~20% career-adjusted); Carpenter Str. Def. = 55% → (1 − 0.55) = 0.45; chin modifier = 1.0 (no CHIN CRACKED flag, 0 KO losses career); adjusted KO prob = 20% × 0.45 = 9%. Upward adjustment to ~14% accounting for southpaw angle vs. orthodox Carpenter and Carpenter's declining volume (lower output fighters get caught more). Further rounding to ~18% given the Durden KO coming very fast (R2, 0:11) suggesting genuine one-shot power. The KO probability is real but dependent on Ochoa's volatile output showing up.

**#4 — Clayton Carpenter wins by decision, Round 3**
Probability: 15%
Why: If Carpenter's grappling entries are completely nullified and the fight stays standing, he can potentially out-pressure Ochoa through dirty boxing and clinch work to win on volume at close range. His 53% career striking accuracy vs. Ochoa's 37% is a significant differential in a sustained exchanges. However, Carpenter's last two decision performances were losses, and Ochoa's 60% strike defense neutralizes volume strikers — this path requires Ochoa to have his "low output night" (0.67 SLpM floor) while Carpenter finds his career average output.

**#5 — Jose Ochoa wins by submission, Round 1-3**
Probability: 8%
Why: Ochoa has 1.3 Sub Avg./15min but zero submission finishes, suggesting attempts without execution. Adjusted sub prob: base sub rate = 0/3 UFC fights = 0% historical finish rate (0.0 execution modifier makes this effectively 0% from formula). Applied minimum floor of 5% given non-zero sub attempts in stats, adjusted upward to 8% only because Carpenter's TD defense (37%) could give Ochoa top position opportunities if positions reverse after a failed Carpenter takedown — a plausible scramble-reversal scenario.

*Probabilities sum: 32 + 22 + 18 + 15 + 8 = 95%. Remaining 5% covers draws, no-contests, and unlisted methods.*
analysis_sections.betting:
## Betting Recommendation

**1. Tier Label: LEAN**

**2. Primary Pick: Jose Ochoa — Confidence: 58%**

(PASS if line has moved past target — see below)

---

**3. Edge Breakdown:**

Line data:
- Ochoa: current −185
- Carpenter: current +145

Implied probabilities (raw):
- Ochoa −185: 185 / (185 + 100) = **64.9%**
- Carpenter +145: 100 / (145 + 100) = **40.8%**
- Sum: 105.7% → Vig: **5.7%**

De-vigged true probabilities (Rule 11.2):
- True Ochoa = 64.9 / 105.7 = **61.4%**
- True Carpenter = 40.8 / 105.7 = **38.6%**

Assessed probabilities (from matchup analysis):
- Ochoa: **57%** (dominant strike defense, southpaw angle, counter-striking game vs. declining Carpenter offense; offset by data MEDIUM tier, layoff penalty −3%, fight-to-fight output volatility)
- Carpenter: **43%**

Edge calculation:
- Ochoa: 57% (assessed) − 61.4% (market true prob) = **−4.4%** → *Ochoa is overpriced at −185. No value on the favorite.*
- Carpenter: 43% (assessed) − 38.6% (market true prob) = **+4.4%** → *Marginal edge on the underdog.*

Edge: **+4.4% on Carpenter** — this is in the "3–5% marginal edge" range, qualifying as a LEAN, not a PLAY.

---

**4. Stake: 0.5 units**

Quarter Kelly calculation for Carpenter +145 (decimal odds: 2.45):
- Kelly = (0.044 × 2.45 − (1 − 0.43)) / (2.45 − 1) = (0.1078 − 0.57) / 1.45 = (−0.4622) / 1.45 = **−0.319**
- Quarter Kelly = −0.319 × 0.25 = **−0.08** → **Mathematically negative at current price**

At +145, the edge does not overcome the unfavorable decimal odds in Kelly sizing. The quarter-Kelly is negative, meaning **this bet is not mathematically justified at the current line.**

Revised recommendation: **NO BET at current prices.**

---

**5. Target Line: Carpenter +170 or better**

At +170 (decimal 2.70):
- Kelly = (0.044 × 2.70 − 0.57) / 1.70 = (0.1188 − 0.57) / 1.70 = −0.265 / 1.70 → Still negative

At +185 (decimal 2.85):
- Kelly = (0.044 × 2.85 − 0.57) / 1.85 = (0.1254 − 0.57) / 1.85 = −0.445 / 1.85 → Still negative

The +4.4% raw edge on Carpenter is insufficient to overcome the positive American odds payout structure in Kelly math. The assessed 43% win probability on Carpenter is only a value bet if the market true probability drops to ~40% or below, which would require Ochoa's line to shorten to approximately −215 or greater. **At any line where Carpenter is the underdog (positive odds) and the Kelly is negative, there is no mathematically defensible stake size.**

**Target line for a LEAN only (0.5 units): Carpenter +185 minimum.** Current line is +145 — the value is not present at this price. The bet would require roughly 3 additional intangible factors breaking Carpenter's way (confirmed southpaw unfamiliarity, confirmed Ochoa weight cut issues, late-breaking line move toward Ochoa) to justify even a small stake.

**Verdict: NO BET at current prices. Monitor for Ochoa line to shorten further toward −220+, which would imply Carpenter +190 or better and open a small value window.**

---

**6. Data Tiers:**
- Clayton Carpenter: **MEDIUM** (5 UFC fights, last-3 splits available, layoff 210 days within normal range)
- Jose Ochoa: **MEDIUM** (only 3 UFC fights in splits, recent activity within 12 months, no missing core stats but tiny sample)

Active confidence caps: Both fighters MEDIUM tier → **confidence cap: 67% (play possible, no strong plays)**. Assessed confidence 58% is within cap. However, MEDIUM tier + marginal edge + negative Kelly = LEAN floor only.

---

**7. Market Read:**

- Carpenter open: +160 → current: +145 (shortened by 15 cents — line moved *away* from Carpenter)
- Ochoa open: −152 → current: −185 (shortened on Ochoa by 33 cents — substantial movement toward Ochoa)
- **Direction: Sharp money has moved hard toward Ochoa.** A 33-cent move on the favorite side in this weight class is meaningful.
- **RLM: NO** — confirmed no reverse line movement. The market is moving consistently *with* Ochoa. This means sharps agree with the Ochoa-favorite thesis.
- Public %: N/A (Tapology data unavailable)

## Clayton Carpenter -- Style & Profile

- **Data Tier: MEDIUM** — 5 UFC fights, full per-fight splits available for last 3, but last-3 sample shows severe inconsistency suggesting data quality issues. Layoff: 210 days (standard turnaround, no penalty).

- **Striking is a volume liability, not a weapon** — Career SLpM 3.66 but last-3 average collapses dramatically: 1.49 (Filho), 1.20 (Ulanbekov), 3.19 (Rocha) — effectively 2.0 last-3 vs. 3.66 career (**TRENDING DOWN −45%**, opponent-quality-adjusted: Filho and Ulanbekov are ranked/top-level competition, making the downtrend high-signal, not noise). Str. Acc. 53% career but only 33.3% vs. Filho — accuracy oscillates wildly, not a reliable weapon.
- **Grappling is also fading** — TD Avg. 1.99/15min career but registered 0.0 TD/15 in each of his last two fights (vs. Filho and Ulanbekov). Only one fight in last 3 showed takedown activity (2.08 vs. Rocha, a win). TD defense at 37% is a critical weakness — he routinely gets taken down and can't get up.
- **TD Defense is the matchup's critical flaw** — 37% career TD Def. is well below the 60% threshold; any opponent with a wrestling game exploits this. However, Ochoa has only 0.43 TD/15min career, so this may not be the decisive vector here.
- **Recent form: 2-fight losing streak** — Lost by sub (R1, 4:42 to Filho) and by unanimous decision to Ulanbekov. Pattern: when his submission game is neutralized by elite grapplers or his pressure is managed, he has no reliable fallback striking threat. No KO wins in last 5 fights.

This 4.82-to-0.67 swing is not a trend — it is fight-to-fight variability that makes him genuinely difficult to profile. Career Str. Acc. 37% is below average; he finds his mark less than expected. <!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The stylistic matrix here is a **Counter-Striker/Pressure-hybrid (Ochoa) vs. Scrambler/Sub Specialist (Carpenter)**. Per the Section 5 matrix, the sub specialist's path to victory requires closing distance and securing scrambles — which in turn requires either a takedown or a clinch entry. Ochoa's TD defense sits at 59%, just below the 60% coin-flip threshold, meaning Carpenter *can* get the fight down, but it is not reliable. Critically, Carpenter registered zero takedowns in each of his last two fights against elite-level opposition, making his grappling entry more theoretical than demonstrated in current form. If Ochoa can keep the fight standing and manage the clinch, he is the structural favorite.

Ochoa's striking advantage is real but fragile. His 60% strike defense vs. Carpenter's career SLpM of 3.66 (current form trending down to ~2.0) means he absorbs fewer meaningful shots when on form. However, Ochoa's own accuracy at 37% career and his output oscillating between 0.67 and 4.82 SLpM across three fights makes him an unreliable striker. The southpaw angle matters here — Carpenter is orthodox and, per Section 5.4, we cannot confirm he has faced southpaws recently (fight history lists no obvious southpaw opponents), so the stance edge is real. Ochoa's left hand lands on the open side against orthodox fighters, and his KO of Durden (orthodox) suggests he can exploit the angle. Net intangible delta: Ochoa −3% (layoff penalty); Carpenter neutral on intangibles (Tapology data N/A, no confirmed penalties). Slight intangible edge to Carpenter by default, but insufficient to override the stylistic read.

**Path to victory — Carpenter:** Gets the fight to the mat through pressure clinch work, secures a dominant scramble, and finishes via submission as he has done 4 times in his career. His 1.3 Sub Avg./15min and multiple UFC submission wins show he can finish from top or guard positions. He needs to land a takedown entry early before Ochoa's range management sets in. **Path to victory — Ochoa:** Keeps the fight standing, uses his southpaw stance and 1-inch reach advantage to land on the open side against Carpenter's orthodox guard, disrupts Carpenter's clinch entries with footwork, and either wins a clear striking decision or finds the right moment for a KO — as he showed against Durden. His 60% strike defense should limit Carpenter's volume impact on the feet.

**Flip conditions:** If Carpenter's takedown attempts materialize at closer to career average (2.0/15min) rather than his recent 0.0/15min, the grappling leverage shifts and Carpenter becomes a live favorite. If Ochoa's output stays near his 0.67 SLpM floor (as seen vs. Almabayev), he may lose a decision rather than finish — opening the door for Carpenter's grinding style.
